Tafseer: Although the address "Ya ayyuha an-nas" (O mankind) is general, the implication refers to those rebellious Quraysh who were denying the Resurrection and making haste for the punishment. It is said: "Fear your Lord, value the respite He has given out of His grace, and benefit from it." Although He is slow to punish because of His mercy and kindness, He is also extremely severe in grasp. Do not consider the Resurrection an easy thing, that you are demanding it with such defiance. Its commotion will be extremely terrifying. It is something to seek refuge from, not something to demand!

A picture of the terror of Resurrection:
This is a picture of the terror of that day, showing that it will be a day of such extreme self-concern that no one's helpers, brothers, or close relatives will be of any use to them. On that day, a nursing mother, to whom her child is dearer than life, will forget her child, and every pregnant woman will drop her carriage due to terror. The state of the people will be such that they will appear completely intoxicated and senseless. But this intoxication will not be from the influence of wine; rather, the terror of divine punishment will make everyone lose their minds!

In the verse, both the plural and singular pronouns are used simultaneously for the addressee. We have explained in another place that when the singular pronoun is used for a group, it refers to every single individual of the group personally, and this carries far more emphasis than the plural.

A specific style of expression:
In the style of expression "Wa min an-nasi man..." (And among the people is he who...), when something is said, the purpose is to mention the specific out of the general. If the context is of praise, this style comes for praise; and if the context is of condemnation, as is the case here, the intensity of condemnation will be highlighted by it. If we want to express this style in our own language, we would say: "Among the people are such ignorant, foolish, and simpleton ones who say this or do that." This specifically mentions that group which was always ready for debate and disputation in opposing the Prophet's call, particularly Monotheism (Tawheed). Such people are not very large in number in any society, but form a specific faction. Their knowledge is based merely on hearsay and rote-learned things, but they are highly adept in loquacity and skilled in boasting. For this reason, they serve well as agents of clever leaders to mislead the simple-minded public.

Disputation without knowledge:
"Yujadilu fee Allahi bighayri 'ilmin"—by "fee Allahi" is meant "fee tawheedi Allahi" (regarding the Monotheism of Allah), because the Arab pagans did not deny God. They only denied the Monotheism of God, and to deny Monotheism, they had no argument except the blind imitation of ancestral religion. Forward in verse 8, explanation will come that they possessed neither any knowledge of God's religion, nor any guidance of intellect and nature, nor any Quran or Book, but just like that, without any argument or knowledge, they stood up for debate regarding the Monotheism of God. It should be kept in mind that professional debaters possess no knowledge except loquacity. They only receive inspiration from their active and eloquent leaders and beat their drums in every street.

By "Satan" is meant the devils of both jinn and humans:
"Wa yattabi'u kulla shaytanin mareed"—by "Satan" is meant the devils of both jinn and humans. The word "kull" (every) points to this meaning. There is a very deep alliance between the devils of jinn and the devils of humans. The devils of jinn make the devils of humans the medium for their mischief-making. The devils of jinn inspire, and the devils of humans turn their inspiration into a philosophy under various names, and then spread it through all means to mislead the children of Adam. The Quran has used the word Satan for all such leaders of misguidance.

Meaning of "Mareed":
"Mareed" means rebellious, wicked, and completely devoid of good (la khayra feehi).

Whether they are devils of jinn or devils of humans, they always appear in the garb of well-wishers, advisors, sympathizers, and lovers of the nation and country, but in reality, they are extremely wicked, rebellious, and completely devoid of good. They turn God's servants away from God's path and put them on their own track, and make those who become their heralds and tools in spreading their trials the companions of Hell along with themselves. It is said: "Those who are debating and disputing with Our Prophet regarding the Monotheism of God are followers of such rebellious and good-devoid devils."

The wisdom of mentioning Monotheism alongside Resurrection:
This immediate mention of Monotheism alongside the mention of Resurrection is because, in reality, the entire significance of Resurrection is bound with Monotheism. If the existence of partners and intercessors alongside God is accepted, the entire significance of Resurrection is finished. If there is a Day of Justice, what fear can there be from it if such partners and intercessors exist who can save their followers from God's grasp by their power, influence, effort, or intercession! For this reason, in the Quran, both themes—Resurrection and the refutation of Shirk—are always mentioned together so that the reality may be clarified to the people: that no one will be able to save anyone from God's grasp. Everyone's matter will be presented in His presence alone, and He alone will decide for everyone.

The duty of Satan:
In the Quran, in places, this matter is explained in detail: that Satan obtained respite from Allah to mislead the children of Adam until Resurrection, and Allah gave him this respite: "Go, whomever makes you a friend, helper, and guide, mislead them; I will throw all of them and you into Hell." This reality is expressed here by the words "kutiba 'alayhi" (decreed upon him). That is, this is a determined duty of Satan from God: that whomever takes him as an ally, he should mislead them and guide them toward the path of Hell. "Fa-annahu..." is conjoined to "annahu", and "man tawallahu" is by way of clarifying the condition—meaning, this duty of Satan to mislead people and show them the path of Hell is conditional upon: only those who take him as their ally and guardian will fall into his trap. He will have no power over those servants of God who consider him their enemy and always try to save themselves from his trials. This is exactly like saying: the function of poison is to destroy people; foolish are those who consider it an antidote and swallow it. The respite God gave to Satan is for the trial of people; it is not given for making him a leader, receiving inspiration from him, or following him.

The upcoming theme... Verses 5-24:
Forward, the same theme of Resurrection and Monotheism mentioned in the initial verses is taken in a new color. An argument for Resurrection and Monotheism is established from the nature of human creation, the observations of this universe, and the attributes of the Creator of this universe that come before every wise person through these observations. It is shown that if man ponders, the arguments for Resurrection and Monotheism are present within his own existence and his own surroundings. There is no need to go far for them. Foolish are those who deny such obvious realities and argue about them. Within this connection, those people are also addressed who wanted to worship God on their own conditions. As far as their assumption was concerned, they avoided those commands of God which they considered contrary to their desires and interests. A group of such double-minded and hypocritical people had emerged in this period on seeing the growing power of Islam, who wished to combine both God and Satan, disbelief and Islam. Although the attitude of this group was apparently tolerant compared to the open adversaries, in reality, this attitude too was Shirk, rather highly disgusting Shirk; therefore, the Quran has struck a blow at it here too, showing that there is no room for this hellish policy in the religion of God. Those who do not accept both benefit and harm to be in God's hand alone, and do not want to worship God alone in every soft and hard situation, may adopt their preferred way, but must remember that no great plan of theirs can be of use to them without the will of God. At the end, the end of both groups of this struggle of truth and falsehood—the believers and the supporters of disbelief and Shirk—is mentioned, showing that a day is coming when the matter of both parties will be presented in God's court, and both will receive punishment or reward according to their respective belief and action. In this light, recite the verses.

Observation of Resurrection within and without:
"Fee raybin mina al-ba'thi" (in doubt about the Resurrection)—the attitude of the Arab polytheists regarding Resurrection was not of clear denial, but of doubt and hesitation. They considered it a highly far-fetched thing, and the main cause for this far-fetchedness in their view was that rising again after dying and turning to dust was highly far-fetched to their imagination. Because of this delusion of theirs, first, they did not give any importance to Resurrection; and even if they believed in it as a hypothesis, they relied on their assumed partners and intercessors to save them from God's questioning through their intercession. When the Quran mentioned the Resurrection with such force and emphasis that it became the actual purpose of this life and this universe, and at the same time proved all their partners and intercessors to be nonentities, this thing was highly hard upon them. They felt that their entire building was established on sand. To save it, their only weapon was to demand, to corner the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "If Resurrection is such a certain thing, show it to us, or show some such physical sign in the form of punishment after which no room for doubt remains regarding the occurrence of Resurrection." The Quran kept this mindset of theirs in view and directed them toward their own existence and the daily observations of this world, showing: there is no need to go far to search for the argument of Resurrection and Monotheism; rather, God has made man and this universe in such a manner that anyone who has eyes can observe God and Resurrection within himself and without at all times.

Meaning of "'Alaqah":
"Fa-inna khalaqnakum min turabin thumma min nutfatin thumma min 'alaqatin thumma min mudghatin mukhaliaqatin wa ghayri mukhaliaqatin linubayyina lakum"—"'alaqah" means a clot of blood, and also a small living worm. This is the stage after the drop of semen settles, when it takes the form of blood and an embryo, and the manifestation of life occurs in it.

Meaning of "Mudghah":
"Mudghah" refers to a lump of flesh. This is the stage after "'alaqah", when the embryo takes the form of a body in the shape of a lump of flesh. Regarding this "mudghah", it is said that some of them are fully fashioned in design, while some are left unfinished by power, and it is in no one's capability to complete them.

Man's own creation is a place of lesson:
"Linubayyina lakum" (that We may make clear to you)—this is the actual purpose for which attention is drawn to these stages of human creation. The meaning is: God was not in need of these stages and all this arrangement to bring man into existence—that a drop of water settle in some womb, then take the form of blood and embryo, and power carry out its painting and sculpting according to its design to make it a beautiful figure. Without all this arrangement, if God wanted, fully-formed men could have emerged in groups from some river or mountain; but God willed that man's own creation become a place of lesson for him to recognize the power, wisdom, and providence of his Creator. His own existence should testify that He who created him with this arrangement and care has not made him as a mere toy; rather, behind his creation is a great purpose which will surely manifest. From his every hair, he should obtain the testimony: the Creator created him from the essence of clay and a drop of water, and faced no difficulty in it, so raising him again is also highly easy for Him. This exact same theme is stated in Surah Al-Mu'minun in this manner:
(And certainly We created man from an extract of clay. Then We placed him as a sperm-drop in a firm lodging. Then We made the sperm-drop into a clinging clot, and We made the clot into a lump [of flesh], and We made [from] the lump, bones, and We covered the bones with flesh; then We developed him into another creation. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators. Then indeed, after that you are to die. Then indeed you, on the Day of Resurrection, will be resurrected.) [Al-Mu'minun: 12-16]

In this verse of Surah Al-Mu'minun, that conclusion is placed forward to which these stages of human creation are testifying, but this is a strange blindness of our modern philosophers and scientists: they see all these stages and ranks of human creation, but their sight does not reach that actual reality for whose explanation God made all this arrangement.

"Wa nuqirru fee al-arhami ma nasha'u ila ajalin musamman thumma nukhrijukum tiflan"—the actual connection of speech was not yet completed; in the middle, the segment "linubayyina lakum" came suddenly to shake the addressees, lest they remain asleep due to simpletonness. After shaking them, the actual connection of speech was taken up again. It is said: "After that, We lodge it in the womb for a term to take the shape of a complete child. 'Ma nasha'u'—meaning, this depends entirely on Our will: whether it be a boy or a girl, beautiful or ugly, imperfect or perfect; it is in no one else's power to make a girl a boy or an ugly one beautiful. 'Ila ajalin musamman'—similarly, We have prescribed a term for childbirth, and this too is entirely in Our power; no one can change the term prescribed by Us."

"Thumma litablughoo ashuddakum"—here, "Li" is a clue that the verb is omitted before it. Many examples of this have passed, and a very clear example of this will come in Surah Ghafir, verse 67. That is: "After giving you existence in the form of a child, We give you a time to reach your maturity and strength, and this law too is made by Us; no one can leap over the stages of childhood and adolescence to step into the limits of youth."

"Wa minkum man yutawaffa wa minkum man yuraddu ila ardhal al-'umuri"—here, on the principle of opposition, based on contextual clarity, in the first segment, words like "fee sibaahu aw fee shababihi" (in his youth or childhood) are omitted, and in the second segment, the words "ardhal al-'umuri" (the most decrepit age) clarify this omission. The meaning is: "How many of you die in your childhood or youth, and how many are those who reach the final stage of old age, and this too is entirely in God's power. No one can decrease or increase their span of life."

"Li" is in the meaning of end and limit:
"Likayla ya'lama min ba'di 'ilmin shay'an"—"Li" is in the meaning of end and limit. Examples of this have passed. Its translation "until" or "so that" is correct in our view. The indefinite form of "'ilm" (knowledge) can be for insignificance or for highlighting. In the first form, the meaning is: he reaches such a decrepit age that whatever little knowledge he had acquired also disappears. In the second form, the meaning is: after knowing many things, he then knows nothing; everything is erased from his memory. We prefer the first meaning because the parallels in the Quran support it. He said: (And you were not given of knowledge except a little) [Al-Isra: 85]. The actual reality is that man, even if he becomes Socrates or Hippocrates, carries only a drop of the boundless ocean of knowledge; he does not possess the capacity in his nature for more than this, and the state of this knowledge too is such that in a specific part of life, it is all snatched away, from which this reality is clarified: no one should be proud of their knowledge. This too is the gift of God; He gives it and He can snatch it away whenever He wills.

A look at the external world:
"Wa tara al-arda hamidatan fa-idha anzalna 'alayha al-ma'a ihtazzat wa rabat wa anbatat min kulli zawjin baheej"—after drawing attention to the arguments of Resurrection present within the creation of man, he is directed toward the external world: "You see the earth completely dry and barren. Then, when rain falls upon it, it becomes soft and sways, and brings out every kind of beautiful vegetation." The meaning is: when you see this earth dying and living repeatedly, why do you consider your own rising again from this earth far-fetched! In every season, this rehearsal is happening before you for this very purpose: that you may observe the Resurrection before its occurrence. The words "linubayyina lakum" are omitted here. Since this sign was completely clear, there was no need to repeat it.

Verses 6-7:
Summary of the discussion:
What has been stated from the beginning of the Surah until here, its summary is placed forward in these verses. That is: "This direction being given to you to fear God and the Resurrection is because the True Deity is God alone; besides Him, those whom you have made partners and on whose strength you are carefree of God and the Hereafter have no reality—they are merely the creation of your imagination. 'Wa annahu yuhyee al-mawta'—meaning, from the arguments mentioned above, it is proved that God revives the dead; therefore, when He wills, He will raise you. He is powerful over everything. He who makes a wise and mature man from a drop of water and makes the earth green and fresh after its dryness—why should reviving the world anew be difficult for Him!"

Disputation without argument is pride:
This is the detail of the attitude of those disputers pointed to in verse 3. There we have pointed out that the main dispute of the polytheists regarding God was with the doctrine of Monotheism. As far as God is concerned, they believed in Him without debate, but along with Him, they had made many other partners, the responsibility to prove which lay upon themselves. But they had no argument for this. The maximum they presented was: "Our ancestors worshipped these deities, so we will worship them and will not tolerate their insult under any circumstances." It is obvious that when the connection of discussion remains with blind imitation of ancestors instead of argument, this is pride and arrogance, falling into which all logic becomes useless to man. For this reason, the Quran has declared this disputation without argument as pride:
(Indeed, those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without any authority having come to them—there is nothing in their breasts except pride, [the extent of] which they cannot reach.) [Ghafir: 56]

In another place, this reality is also clarified: the motive for all this disputation is the blind bias of the ancestral way:
(And among the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge or guidance or an enlightening Book. And when it is said to them, "Follow what Allah has revealed," they say, "Rather, we will follow that upon which we found our fathers.") [Luqman: 20-21]

"Thaniya 'itfihi" is a picture of their pride and arrogance. When someone turns his face from someone with arrogance, he turns it shaking his shoulders. When a person has no argument, yet is not ready to withdraw from his wrong position, his pride suffers a blow, and he tries to take revenge for it by displaying his arrogance. "Liyadilla 'an sabeeli Allahi"—meaning, all this pomp and all this zeal is not for the support of some truth, but only so that, just as he himself is astray from God's path, he may mislead others too. It should be kept in mind: a person being astray from God's path, if he feels his weaknesses, is not something that makes one hopeless of him; the possibility of his return to the truth is there, but one who, despite having no argument in favor of his falsehood, stands up to impose it on others with complete arrogance—no room for any hope of good is left in him.

The punishment of arrogance is humiliation in this world and the Hereafter:
"Lahu fee ad-dunya khizyun wa nudheequhu yawma al-qiyamah 'adhaba al-hareeq"—it is stated: "For such people is humiliation in this world and the punishment of fire in the Hereafter." Humiliation because they displayed arrogance against the truth; therefore, they are worthy of being humiliated in this world too. It should be kept in mind that those under discussion here are the opponents of the messenger. In the case of the opponents of the messengers, the Divine Sunnah, as we have explained in various places, is this: if they persist in opposing the truth, after the completion of proof, they inevitably face defeat and humiliation in this world, and in the Hereafter too, they will be worthy of Hell.

Within "'adhab al-hareeq" (the punishment of burning), the aspect of similarity between action and reward is present. That is: since they kept burning and boiling with anger, hatred, and jealousy against the truth in this world, they are worthy of tasting the punishment of burning in the Hereafter.

The testimony of the state:
We have been explaining this style of the Quran in places: where the purpose is to describe the situation, verbs like "qila" (it was said) or "yuqalu" (it is said) are generally omitted. Here too, the same is the case. That is, the state itself will testify against them: "This is the harvest of your own sown poisonous crop which has come before you. God has not done any injustice to you."

As you sow, so shall you reap:
"Wa anna Allaha laysa bidallamin lil-'abeed"—the explanation of this style has passed in places: when negation comes on exaggeration, the purpose is exaggeration in negation. For this reason, "laysa bidallam" means: Allah is not unjust to His servants in the least.

The purpose of the verse is: whatever man sows in this world, he will reap its harvest in the Hereafter. If he sowed arrogance, the harvest of arrogance is humiliation—he will reap the harvest of humiliation; and if he nurtured anger and hatred against the truth, its harvest is the punishment of burning—he will face it. In short, whatever he did, Allah will place before him. Not a hair's breadth of injustice will be done to him!

The character of the double-minded:
After mentioning those hardcore polytheists who were waging war in support of Shirk, now comes the mention of those who wished to maintain connection with both God and Satan, Allah and other than Allah, truth and falsehood, at the same time. They did not want to join either the believers or the polytheists with single-mindedness; they stood apart from both ranks to look after their interest and benefit. To the extent they saw benefit in accompanying the Muslims, they accompanied them, and when they saw benefit in supporting Shirk and its people, they became their companions. A group of such double-faced Muslims had emerged in this period, seeing the growing power of Islam, among the polytheists and the People of the Book, who wished for a compromise between disbelief and Islam. They said: "God should be worshipped, and these idols and deities too whose worship has been coming down from ancestors. After all, they too are helpful entities, so why should they be ignored?" This is the very group of whose proposal to alter the Quran mention has passed in Surah Yunus. If such elements entered among the Muslims, they did not become single-minded in Monotheism. As far as they saw the path smooth, they accompanied the caravan of Islam, but when any trial presented itself, they stumbled there, and becoming hopeless and bad-thinking of God, made others their ally and refuge. This thing, in its reality, is Shirk. If there is any difference between the Shirk of the disputers mentioned above and this, it is not of reality but only of temperament. The temperament of the disputers' Shirk is adversarial; the temperament of this is hypocritical. Apart from this difference, there is no other difference between the two. The demand of Monotheism is fulfilled only when the servant hands himself over to his Lord entirely. Whether He lays him on a bed of flowers, he remains pleased; and if saws run on his head, he remains pleased and content. One who wants to worship God only to the extent of benefit is not a monotheist but a polytheist, and there is no place in God's presence for such opportunists and interest-seekers. Sarmad said in a highly two-to-one manner:
"Sarmad, a short complaint must be made,
One of these two works must be done:
Either the body must be given to His pleasure,
Or the sight must be turned away from the Friend."

"Wa mina an-nasi man ya'budu Allaha 'ala harfin"—this style of expression here is for expressing surprise, dislike, and aversion, and the word "'ibadah" (worship) contains the meaning of both worship and obedience. "'Ala harfin"—meaning on an edge, standing far away, without entering fully into the worship and obedience of God, which is the demand of "udkhuloo fee as-silmi kaffah"; rather, becoming the referent of "mudhabdabina bayna dhalika la ila ha'ula'i wa la ila ha'ula'i"—neither turning fully to God, which is the status of a upright Muslim, nor like the disbelievers in supporting disbelief openly, but "turning the face here and there, stretching the hand."

"Fa-in asabahu khayrun itma'anna bihi wa-in asabat-hu fitnatun inqalaba 'ala wajhihi"—"fitnah" here means calamity and trial. This is the explanation of "'ala harfin": these foolish people want to worship God only to the extent of their interest. As long as every matter is according to their desire, they remain pleased and content with God, but if any trial presents itself, they turn upside down and begin to rub their foreheads on the thresholds of others, leaving God. They are seekers of flowers but not ready to tolerate the prick of thorns, whereas in God's path, along with comforts are trials too, and until one stands successful in these trials, there is no inquiry of him in God's presence.

Meaning of "Khayr ad-dunya wal-akhirah":
"Khasira ad-dunya wal-akhirata"—it is stated: "Such foolish people ruin both this world and the Hereafter." If some trial presents itself to the servant in this world, and he is tested with some loss of life or wealth, a great benefit of the Hereafter is hidden for him inside this loss, on the condition that he remains pleased and content with his Lord's decision and casts himself before Him. Every such trial makes the servant's faith mature and multiplies his benefits of the Hereafter; but if some fool becomes frightened by such a trial and becomes hopeless and bad-thinking of God, he suffered the loss of this world and also ruined his Hereafter. "Dhalika huwa al-khusranu al-mubeen"—it is obvious that the true and open loss is this. If some loss of this world occurred, it is not a loss if the servant secured his reward of the Hereafter in its exchange. But one who lost his world too and his Hereafter too—his loss is an open loss.

The character of the sons of the age:
In "Wa mina an-nasi man ya'budu Allaha 'ala harfin", whose character is described—if you ponder, you will realize that this is the very character of the sons of the age. Today, we too, who were appointed to eradicate this Shirk, want to fulfill the right of God's worship from far away in this manner. We believe in the Quran as the Book of God too, but in law, civilization, society, economy, and politics, we follow others in every sphere of life. The use of the Quran is now only this: when someone dies, some memorizers recite the Quran to gift its reward to him, although he himself might have never touched the Quran even by chance! In their view, the Quran was sent down not for the living but for the dead, and not for guidance but only to be kissed sometimes! How well said by whoever said it:
"Friends have a strange double-faced style,
They have the Quran under their arm, but the religion of the Westerner."

Calling upon other than God is Shirk and far-off error:
"Yad'oo" (he calls upon) here contains the meaning of calling, seeking help, crying out, seeking mercy, and seeking support. If anyone is called upon, accepting them as beneficial or harmful besides God, this is Shirk. It is stated: "These people, becoming hopeless of God, seek help from those who can neither harm them nor benefit them." Benefit and harm are in God's power alone. If anyone receives any benefit or harm from anyone, they receive it only by God's permission; therefore, in every state, the servant's reliance must be upon Allah alone, not upon others, becoming hopeless of Him. The reality of others being beneficial or harmful is explained through a parable at the end of this Surah:
(They will never create a fly, even if they gathered for it. And if the fly should steal away from them a [tiny] thing, they could not recover it from him. Weak are the pursuer and pursued.) [Al-Hajj: 73]

"Wa dhalika huwa ad-dalalu al-ba'eed"—it is stated: "The far-off error, in reality, is this." The meaning is: if they had angry-turned from God to seek refuge in someone who could bring some benefit or harm to them, this would have been an error, but not very far-off; but to run away from God's trial to seek refuge in those who cannot even ward off a fly from their own faces is not merely an error, but a very far-off error. They fled from the furnace of God's trial and fell straight into Hell!

He calls upon him whose harm is closer than his benefit. Evil indeed is the patron, and evil indeed is the associate!

Regarding this "yad'oo", the school of those grammarians seems strong to me who consider it a repetition of the previously mentioned "yad'oo" and completely unrelated to the subsequent sentence. The repetition of the verb here is for expressing sorrow and regret. "Laman darruhu..." is not the object of "yad'oo" but is a kind of correction (istidrak) on the above object "ma la yadarruhu wa ma la yanfa'uhu", and the purpose of it is to explain its being a far-off error. The meaning is: "These people whom they call upon—not only can they bring no harm or benefit, but, calamity upon calamity, their harm is closer than their benefit." If refuge is sought in someone who can bring neither benefit nor harm, this is foolishness; but the foolishness upon foolishness is to seek refuge in someone whose harm is cash, proved, and known, but benefit is completely imaginary. Those who broke connection with God to take others as their ally and helper destroyed their true support—whether other assumed supports are of use or not is a subsequent thing, and this too will come before them.

Both leader and follower are insolent:
"Labi'sa al-mawla wa labi'sa al-'asheer"—this phrase here is exactly like "da'ufa at-talibu wal-matloobu" (weak are the pursuer and pursued) in verse 73, whose reference has passed above. Just as it is said there: "both pursuer and pursued are helpless and weak", similarly here it is said: "both patron and associate are insolent, one worse than the other." By "mawla" is meant those partners and intercessors who were made the refuge and helper besides God, and by "'asheer" is meant those polytheists who made them the refuge and helper. Master and disciple, leader and follower, if both are cast in the same mold and excel each other in being evil, their end is known!

The end of the sincere:
After describing the end of those who seek the refuge of others, becoming hopeless of God, this points to the end of those who will remain firm on the path of faith and righteous action in every kind of hard, soft, grief, and comfort situation. It is said: "Indeed, Allah will admit those who believe and do righteous deeds into gardens beneath which rivers flow." The context of speech is proof that here the verb is used in its complete meaning, because it is in contrast to "Wa mina an-nasi man ya'budu Allaha 'ala harfin"; for this reason, those who are not involved in double-mindedness and hypocrisy but are firm on their Lord's worship with complete single-mindedness are inevitably meant by this.

"Inna Allaha yaf'alu ma yureed" (Indeed, Allah does what He intends)—meaning, these polytheists, those on whom they rely, are neither capable of bringing any benefit nor any harm, to the extent that if a fly should steal away anything from them, they are not capable of recovering it. However, Allah is powerful over everything; He does whatever He wills. In this is comfort and glad tidings for the Muslims: "Do not look at the unfavorableness of apparent situations. No matter how unfavorable and unhelpful the situations may be, remain firm on your Lord, and remain assured that He can do everything."

The referent of the object pronoun:
"Man kana yadunnu an lan yansurahu Allahu fee ad-dunya wal-akhirati..."—in "yansurahu", the referent of the pronoun is "man" (whoever). Those who have accepted its referent as the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) have an opinion completely unsuited to the context of speech. The pointer in the verse is to those very double-minded and hypocritical people whose mention has been coming down from above, and whose state is described: if any trial presents itself to them, they become hopeless and bad-thinking of God, making others their ally and refuge, and assuming that God will not deliver them from the presented difficulty.

Meaning of "Madd as-sabab":
"Falyamdud bisababin ila as-sama'i"—stretching a rope to the sky is a metaphor for carrying out the final and extreme plan, just as we have the metaphor of applying a patch. In Surah Al-An'am too, the metaphor of applying a patch in the sky has passed. He said:
(And if their evasion is difficult for you, then if you are able to seek a tunnel into the earth or a stairway into the sky to bring them a sign, [then do so]. But if Allah had willed, He would have joined them upon guidance. So never be of the ignorant.) [Al-An'am: 35]

Zuhair and Al-A'sha have also used this idiom for extreme and final struggle. Zuhair's hemistich is:
"And even if he reached the means of the sky with a ladder..."
Similarly Al-A'sha says:
"And you ascended the means of the sky with a ladder..."

Meaning of "Qat'":
"Thumma liyaqta'"—by "yaqta'", Abu Muslim has meant the crossing of distance—meaning, he should stretch a rope to the sky and ascend to the sky. Some have meant the cutting off of revelation—meaning, whoever has this assumption that Allah will not help His Messenger, he should ascend to the sky and cut off the chain of revelation. Most people have meant hanging or strangling oneself—meaning, he should hang a rope from the ceiling and hang himself. Apart from the defect in these interpretations, the meaning of "qat'" these gentlemen have taken is itself a matter of review. For the meaning of cutting off revelation or hanging oneself, the use of this word is completely inappropriate—the taste of Arabic rejects it; using an inappropriate word for some meaning while well-known and current words exist is completely contrary to the eloquence of the Quran. Although for the meaning of crossing distance, this word cannot be declared inappropriate, because idioms like "qata'a al-wadi" (he crossed the valley) are well-known in Arabic, yet using this word for this meaning here is completely inappropriate. The reason for this is: when the stretching of a rope toward the sky is mentioned, along with it, "falyattasa'ad" (let him ascend) or its synonym can be appropriate. "Yaqta'" is not appropriate with it under any circumstances.

In our view, all these statements are weak. However, for the meaning of deciding a matter with determination, this word is well-known in high Arabic. Its parallel is present in the Quran itself. For example, in Surah An-Naml, it is said:
(She said, "O chiefs, advise me in my affair. I would not decide a matter until you are present [and testify].") [An-Naml: 32]

The end of hopelessness and bad-thinking of God:
After explaining the parts of the verse, now ponder over the meaning of the verse. Its meaning is: the wretched and unsuccessful one who, becoming hopeless of God, is bad-thinking that He will not help him, and falling into this bad-thinking, makes others his ally and refuge, may do whatever he wishes—let him stretch a rope in the sky and use all his strength to decide his matter, and if he can remove his grief and worry, let him remove it. The meaning is: God has no concern with such good-devoid ones. They may wander wherever they wish and try to quench their thirst from whichever puddle they wish. But they must remember: the thirst present inside human nature cannot be quenched by the water of every puddle—it is quenched only by the Kauthar of faith; and the vacuum present in it cannot be filled by every brick and stone—if it can be filled, it can be filled only by the remembrance of the One God. Take a look at what we have written under verse 124 of Surah Ta-Ha "Wa man a'rada 'an dhikree fa-inna lahu ma'eeshatan dankan", too. Some further questions that will arise regarding this verse will become clear from it. It should be kept in mind here: hopelessness and bad-thinking of God are among the greatest factors of Shirk. We have explained this reality in our book 'The Reality of Shirk'. When man cuts off from God, he, forced by his nature, tries to find some support, but since the true support is God and God alone, no matter if he ascends to the sky, all his effort remains unproductive.

In this verse, the phrase "fee ad-dunya wal-akhirati" is also worthy of consideration. The meaning is: just as in this world God alone is the Helper and Protector, similarly in the Hereafter too God alone is the Helper and Protector. If anyone, becoming hopeless of God's mercy or justice, makes someone else his helper and protector, assuming they will be of use to him against God, let him do this too. The reality of this delusion of his will be displayed to him on the Day of Resurrection.

This verse serves as a warning, and "kadhalika" points to those clear arguments of Monotheism and the refutation of Shirk mentioned above. It is said: "In this manner, with highly clear arguments, We have sent down the Quran." After this, based on context, this theme is omitted: those favored with success will believe in the verses, and those deprived of success will, as pointed out in verse 8, continue to argue and dispute regarding Allah and His verses. "Wa anna Allaha yahdee man yureed" points to that Divine Sunnah which Allah has prescribed for guidance and error, and whose explanation we have done repeatedly: guidance from Allah is received only by those who use their intellect and insight; those who become blind and deaf have their hearts sealed.

Warning to the polytheists and their allies:
In this verse, among the groups mentioned, except the Magians (Majus) and the polytheists, every group has been mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 62 too. There we have discussed the Jews, Christians, and Sabians in detail. See pages 182-187, Volume I. The Magians were originally followers of the religion of Zoroaster, and the basis of the religion of Zoroaster, as we have explained in Surah Al-Kahf, was pure Monotheism, but just as with the lapse of time, the shape of every religion became distorted, similarly the followers of this religion also distorted their original religion and fell into fire-worship and other polytheistic innovations.

Regarding the debate and disputation mentioned in the previous verses between the believers and the polytheists, this verse is also related to it. The main parties of this debate were indeed the Muslims and the polytheists of Quraysh, but in this period, as detailed in the previous surahs, the followers of other religions present in Arabia also became parties in it, and since the target of the call of Islam was upon all of them, for this reason, their sympathies were with the polytheists. Particularly, the Jews and Christians were openly supporting them on every front. The Quran informed all of them by name: the front which is hot today regarding God and His verses will not end here, but this dispute will also be presented in God's court on the Day of Resurrection, in which on one hand will be the believers, and on the other hand, these polytheists and their allies—the Jews, Sabians, Christians, and Magians. On that day, God will decide who is on the truth and who is on falsehood; it is obvious that by decision here is meant to point to its result: on that day, the results of this effort and activity in which everyone is engaged today will come before them. The believers will receive the reward of their sacrifices, and the disbelievers, polytheists, and their allies will suffer the consequences of their activities.

"Inna Allaha 'ala kulli shay'in shaheed" (Indeed, Allah is witness over all things)—this is a message of comfort for the believers and a threat and warning for the polytheists and their supporters: that God is observing everything. He is observing the sacrifices and devotion of His believing servants and also the conspiracies and mischief of the opponents. For this reason, He will deal with everyone exactly as they deserve.

Those who are not with the believers are of the disbelievers:
In this verse, you must have noticed: first, the believers are mentioned, and at the end, the polytheists, and in the middle, other various sects. The reason for this, as we have pointed out, is: the status of main parties in this debate and disputation was held only by these two, and the status of other groups was supplementary. Consequently, forward in verse 19, it is specified that the main parties are two: the believers and the people of disbelief and Shirk. Whoever is not with the believers is of the disbelievers, by whatever name he may be called and whichever religion he may claim to follow.

A subtle difference in the use of nouns and verbs:
In the verse, the style of expression adopted for mentioning the different groups is also worthy of consideration. The Muslims, Jews, and polytheists are mentioned in the form of verbs, and the Sabians, Christians, and Magians in the form of nouns. The reason for this is: practically in this field, on one hand were the Muslims, and on the other hand, the polytheists and Jews; the Jews, in their support of the polytheists and opposition to Islam, were highly active due to their jealousy and enmity. The sympathies of the other sects, though they were indeed with the polytheists, were not very active. For this reason, the main adversaries of the battle are mentioned with verbs, and the others with nouns. Those who possess taste for the language know that within the verb, a kind of meaning of activity is found, while the noun generally serves only as a sign of distinction.

Every single thing in the universe is in prostration:
This is that argument of Monotheism whose testimony every single thing in this universe is giving through its existence. We have pointed to this reality in places in this book: every single thing in this universe, in its cosmic status, possesses an Abrahamic temperament. The sun, moon, stars, mountains, and beasts are all subjected under God's command and order. None of these things deviates by a hair's breadth from the laws prescribed by God. The sun, which the simpletons made a deity and worshipped most of all, is testifying through its existence that night and day, it is in standing, bowing, and prostration before its Lord. At the time of dawn, it raises its head from prostration; until noon, it remains in standing; after decline, it bows in bowing; and at the time of sunset, it falls into prostration, and remains in this state of prostration all night. This same reality is demonstrated by the moon through its crescent and waning, and by the stars through their rising and setting. The mountains, trees, and beasts also have the same state. The shadow of every single of these things remains in standing, bowing, and prostration at all times. And if you ponder, this reality will also be seen: the nature of this shadow is so Abrahamic that it always remains in the opposite direction of the sun. If the sun is in the direction of the east, the shadow will spread toward the west; and if it is toward the west, the shadow of every thing will spread toward the east. As if the shadow of every thing is teaching us through its existence that the actual one worthy of prostration is not the sun, but the Creator of the sun.

This argument of Monotheism is of the nature of indications; for this reason, it does not fall under the grasp of logic, but in the sight of those who ponder over the system of the universe, these indications hold great value:
"Who is he who is the recipient of glad tidings, who knows the indication?"

This same argument is stated in Surah Ar-Ra'd thus:
(And to Allah prostrates whoever is within the heavens and the earth, willingly or by compulsion, and their shadows [as well] in the mornings and the afternoons.) [Ar-Ra'd: 15]

We have explained this verse in Surah Ar-Ra'd. Take a look at it. In it, the words "wa dilaluhum bil-ghuduwwi wal-asal" (and their shadows in the mornings and afternoons) express the reality: if some wretched one rebels against prostrating to God in his sphere of choice, his own shadow expresses aversion to him through its testimony of state, and remains firm on its Abrahamic nature, not tolerating under any circumstances the shame of bowing down to anyone other than God.

In Surah An-Nahl, this argument is stated thus:
(Have they not considered what things Allah has created? Their shadows incline to the right and to the left, prostrating to Allah, while they are humble. And to Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of creatures, and the angels, and they are not arrogant.) [An-Nahl: 48-49]

Greatest honor and greatest humiliation:
"Wa katheerun mina an-nasi wa katheerun haqqa 'alayhi al-'adhabu"—meaning, this same is the state of many of those servants of God whose nature is sound. Just as all non-deliberate things of the universe are subject to the command of Allah and prostrating before Him, similarly many of God's servants are those who have subjected their choice and will to the command of Allah, and they do not prostrate to anyone other than their Lord. But many have used this choice and will wrongly, and through their wretchedness of actions, they made this greatest honor which God granted them the cause of the greatest misguidance and the greatest humiliation for themselves. The state of the things of the universe is: despite that Allah created them for the service of man, none of these things is ready to tolerate the shame of prostrating to man; but the state of man's baseness and foolishness is: despite being the chosen of creations and God's successor on earth, he accepts creations lower than himself as deities and bows down to them! It is said: "These are the ones upon whom the punishment has become binding, because they humiliated themselves and also highly depreciated their Lord."

Man's honor is conditional upon character:
"Wa man yuhini Allahu fama lahu min mukrimin inna Allaha yaf'alu ma yasha'u"—this is a pointer to the reality: the honor God granted to man is conditional upon attributes and character. If he fulfills the right of this honor, Allah maintains his honor and increases his ranks; and if he does not appreciate it, He leaves him, in accordance with His Sunnah, to fall into the very ditch in which he wants to fall. In "nuwallihi ma tawalla" [An-Nisa: 115] and other verses of this theme, pointer is made to this Divine Sunnah. Those who come under the target of this Divine Sunnah cannot have anyone else to support them. In this is also comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "No matter if you try a hundred thousand times to deliver them from this ditch of humiliation into which they have fallen, they are not to come out of it. The decision of God's will after this completion of proof is: now they must suffer this blow of humiliation."

"I am singing in one tune, and my lute is singing in another."

From this verse, one thing known is: man's true honor is Monotheism. If he deprives himself of this honor, he becomes lower and more insignificant than the smallest thing of this universe. The second reality clarified by it is: man's harmony with this universe remains only as long as his forehead is not defiled by prostration to anyone other than God. If he accepts this humiliation, he becomes completely unharmonious with this entire universe, like a square peg in a round hole, to the extent that his own shadow also refuses to accompany him. Then, such a discord is born between him and his shadow that the proverb "I am singing in one tune, and my lute is singing in another" becomes true of him.

Verses 19-23:
Detail of the end of both parties, a question and its answer:
Now, the end of both parties is being mentioned, whose mention passed in verse 17 above. There, only this much was pointed out: a day will come when Allah will decide between them; the detail of their end was not mentioned in that verse. In these verses, the detail of their end is coming.

In verse 17, many groups are mentioned, and here the words "hadhani khasmaan" (these two adversaries) have entered. For this reason, this question arises: if the pointer in this verse is to those groups, why was the dual pronoun used for them? Why was the plural pronoun not used? The inclination of the teacher-imam, in the light of Arabic speech, seems to be: if the parties are more than two, both dual and plural pronouns can be used for them. Here, the plural verb "ikhtasamoo" (they disputed) also supports their opinion. But my inclination, as I have pointed out above, is: here the mention of the above groups, ignoring their apparent plurality, is done in terms of their actual reality. Apparently indeed, the Jews, Christians, Sabians, Magians, and polytheists were separate groups, but as far as opposing Monotheism and Islam was concerned, they were all common in this; rather, the temperament of opposition, as we have pointed out above, was indeed separate, but in opposing Islam, according to their respective inclination, all were partners. For this reason, the Quran, in accordance with the principle of "al-kufru millatun wahidah" (disbelief is one single creed), established all of them as one single party, and gave the Muslims the status of an independent party against them, and in this status, mentioned the end of both.

In "ikhtasamoo fee rabbihim" (they disputed concerning their Lord), pointer is made to that very debate and disputation which was hot between the Muslims and their opposing parties regarding Shirk and Monotheism, and whose mention has passed in verses 3 and 8 above. It should be kept in mind here: in this battle of Monotheism and Shirk, the People of the Book, despite claiming to be People of the Book, openly supported the Arab polytheists.

Shirk in its reality is disbelief, compatibility of reward and action:
By "falladhina kafaroo" (so those who disbelieved) is meant all those parties who supported Shirk in this war of Monotheism and Shirk. The reason for expressing Shirk as disbelief has been pointed out in places: Shirk, in its actual reality and end, is disbelief; for this reason, in the Quran, Shirk is also expressed as disbelief. Those who consider Shirk as light compared to disbelief have an opinion completely contrary to the Quran.

Compatibility of reward and action:
"Qutti'at lahum thiyabun min narin"—meaning, in this world, in the passion of opposing the truth, they kept burning in the fire of hatred, anger, jealousy, and revenge, in reward for which, in the Hereafter, a garment of fire will be tailored for them, and boiling water will be poured over their heads which will melt all their inner organs and their outer skins.

"Wa lahum maqami'u min hadeedin kullama aradoo an yakhrujoo minha min ghammin u'eedoo feeha"—"maqami'" means hammers. The meaning is: for their suppression, there will be hammers of iron. If they try to flee from some calamity of Hell, they will be returned to remain in it by suppressing them with these hammers. From the words "min ghammin" (from anguish), the point emerges: there, no one will be able to even imagine fleeing from Hell. At most, if anyone can try, he will try to seek refuge from the intensity of one calamity into another calamity, but this possibility too will not be there. The keepers of Hell will suppress them with hammers of iron.

Expression of the state's language:
"Wa dhooqoo 'adhaba al-hareeq" (And taste the punishment of Burning)—this is the expression of the state's language. That is, the situation itself will proclaim to them: "Now there is no possibility of coming out from here; now remain in it and taste the punishment of burning!" In such places, as we have been pointing out, there is no need to assume some omitted verb like "qila" (it was said). The language of state is more speaking and eloquent than the language of speech. Here, in accordance with the well-known style of Arabic, the conjoined-to is omitted. That is: "ikhsa'oo feeha wa dhooqoo 'adhaba al-hareeq" (remain humiliated in it and taste the punishment of burning). Examples of this have passed.

Here, the aspect of similarity between action and reward is also in view. Those whom the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions exerted all their strength to deliver from the palace of destruction, but they not only were not pleased with coming out of it but also became enemies of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions on the basis that they tried to deliver them—so in the Hereafter, when its reality comes before them, what is the meaning of fleeing from it? What they loved so much in the world, now let them taste its consequence.

Detail of the end of the people of truth:
After describing the end of the opponents of truth, now the end of those is being mentioned who, despite all the opposition of the opponents, remained firm on the position of truth. It is said: "For them will be gardens beneath which rivers flow." The context of speech is proof that here the verb is used in its complete meaning, because it is in contrast to "Wa mina an-nasi man ya'budu Allaha 'ala harfin"; for this reason, those who are firm on their Lord's worship with complete single-mindedness are meant. "Yuhallawna feeha min asawira min dhahabin wa lu'lu'an"—in "lu'lu'an", the accusative is conjoined to the place of "asawir". That is, in that heaven, they will be decorated with bracelets of gold and necklaces of pearls. From the style of expression "wa libasuhum feeha hareerun" (and their garments therein will be silk), the point emerges: in that heaven, their garment will be silk and silk alone—this will be their garment, their covering, their bedding, and other accessories.

With the beloved:
"Wa hudoo ila at-tayyibi mina al-qawli" points to that word of praise and gratitude which will be uttered by the tongues of the people of heaven spontaneously when they see that their Lord fulfilled all the promises He made. In Surah Az-Zumar, verse 74, its reference has come thus: (And they will say, "Praise to Allah, who has fulfilled for us His promise..."). This same song of praise is mentioned in Surah Fatir, verses 34-35: (And they will say, "Praise to Allah, who has removed from us [all] sorrow. Indeed, our Lord is Forgiving and Appreciative—He who has settled us in the home of duration out of His bounty.")

Meaning of "Sirat al-Hameed":
"Wa hudoo ila sirati al-hameedi"—by "sirat al-hameed", in my view, is meant the highway of that home of duration pointed to in the referenced verse of Surah Fatir. Here, the word "hidayah" (guidance) is in the meaning of guidance toward the destination, and the passive voice is proof of honor and respect: that through a group of angels, the guidance of these people toward the highway will be done which will reach them to that home of duration which God, the Praiseworthy (Al-Hameed), has approved for their stay.

Expression of the states of observation of truth through words of wine and chalice:
It should be kept in mind here: the states of heaven and Hell belong to an unseen world. To bring them close to the addressee's mind, there is no way except to adopt that style of expression with which the addressee is familiar. The Arabs were influenced by the civilization of the Egyptians and Persians; for this reason, to express comfort and luxury, that style is adopted. Sometimes, in our own conceptions, a great difference is born with the difference of times. For this reason, instead of words, sight must always be on the reality. Only this must be believed: the people of heaven will obtain these blessings mentioned in the Quran; as for what their actual reality is, their knowledge is with Allah alone. Just as sometimes, for the states and matters of observation of truth, expressions of wine and chalice are adopted, similarly, the states of the Hereafter are expressed through those words and representations which are close to understanding for the addressee.

The upcoming theme... Verses 25-37:
The debate of Monotheism and Shirk which has been coming down from above has reached its peak here. The reason why the Quraysh and their allies had adopted such a severe attitude of enmity in this period was: they understood well that the nature of this struggle of calling to Monotheism and refuting Shirk is not merely a theological and doctrinal dispute, but its blow is also to fall upon that prestige, leadership, and power which they possess due to the custodianship of the Sanctuary. The Quraysh had established their control over the Sanctuary, and their claim was: they alone are the true heirs, custodians, and trustees of this Abrahamic inheritance. The family of Bani Hashim held the office of the key-bearing of the Kaaba, and their leader in that era was Abu Lahab, who was usurping the entire treasury of Refadah and was completely free to deal with it as he wished. It should also be kept in mind: the Quraysh did not possess leadership and power in Makkah alone, but due to being considered the guardians of the Sanctuary, the awe of their religious leadership was established over the entire Arabia. In such situations, how could they tolerate a call which would deprive them of all these interests at once. Consequently, they deprived the Muslims of the presence in the Sanctuary and forced them through oppression and violence to leave the land of Makkah. In these situations, these verses were sent down in which the reality is clarified: for what purpose Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had built this house, what his call was, and what signs and rituals Allah had taught him. This is, as it were, a mirror placed before the Quraysh so that they may look at their faces in it and decide themselves whether they are worthy of this Abrahamic inheritance or are usurpers and traitors! Within this, the encouragement of the Muslims is also done, and they are also informed of those responsibilities which are to fall upon them forward regarding the rituals and signs of this house... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Two forms of the structure of speech:
In this verse, two forms of the structure of speech are possible. One: consider the entire segment from "Inna alladhina kafaroo" until "al-'akifu feehi wal-badi" as an introduction, and establish "wa man yurid feehi..." as the actual subject. Second: in it, assume the predicate—meaning "faqad dalamoo dulman kabeeran" (then they have indeed wronged a great wrong) or its synonym—as omitted. By the rules of Arabic, both forms are correct. The inclination of the teacher-imam is toward the first form, and my inclination is toward the second form. I have kept this in view in the translation, but in the interpretation of the verse...

Pointer to the Quraysh:
...no specific difference will occur in either form.

"Inna alladhina kafaroo wa yasuddoona 'an sabeeli Allahi wal-masjidi al-harami"—this is a pointer to the Quraysh who had denied the call of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and were stopping Muslims through force and violence from God's religion, and in this period, as we have pointed out above, they had also deprived Muslims of the presence in the Sanctuary.

A blow at the prestige of Bani Hashim:
"Alladhee ja'alnahu linnasi sawa'an al-'akifu feehi wal-badi"—this is the definition of the Sacred Mosque: that Allah has kept the rights of the residents of the limits of the Sanctuary and those coming from outside equal. This is a blow at the monopoly of the Quraysh and particularly the prestige of Bani Hashim: that they have established their proprietary-like control over this house of Allah, letting whomever they wish enter it, and depriving whomever they wish of its presence—this is completely unlawful. There is no monopoly of any specific family over this house. Everyone who intends this house for God's worship, Hajj, and circumambulation—whether he be Qurayshi, Hashimi, non-Qurayshi, non-Hashimi, Makkan, non-Makkan, Arab, or non-Arab—no one has the right to impose any restriction on him. If the residents of it have any right and duty, it is only to keep it pure and clean from those impurities from which Allah commanded Hazrat Ibrahim and Ismail (peace be upon them) to keep it pure, and whose details are coming forward; but this is the irony of the Quraysh: they have filled this house of Allah with all those impurities from which He directed to keep it pure, yet they are sitting as its custodians and are stopping those servants of Allah who want to illuminate it anew with its original Abrahamic beauty.

The common meaning of "Al-'Akif wal-Badi", as we have pointed out, is resident and visitor here, but by using the word "'akif" (devotee) for the residents of the Sanctuary, the Quran has directed attention to their actual status: that their status is not of contractors, monopolists, and rulers of God's Sanctuary, but of its devotees and servants. They must make it, like Hazrat Ismail, the center of Hajj and worship for the entire world, and invite all creation to come to benefit from the blessings of this divine threshold.

No monopoly of any family or government can be established over the Sacred Mosque:
From these words, the point emerges clearly: as far as the Sacred Mosque is concerned, no monopoly of any family or any specific government can be established over it. In it, the rights of all Muslims of Arab, non-Arab, East, and West are completely equal. The status of the people of Makkah or their government is not of its owners but of its guardians and servants. Their duty is to keep it pure and clean from every trace of Shirk and innovation, and to provide maximum facilities for those servants of Allah who arrive there for Hajj and sacrifice. Under no circumstances can they deprive any Muslim of any region of the world of its presence, unless it is proved that he is trying to use this house of Allah contrary to those purposes for which Hazrat Ibrahim built it.

The protection of the Sacred Mosque is the common responsibility of all Muslims:
Along with rights, the responsibility for the protection and preservation of this house is also shared equally by the Muslims of the entire world. If it is proved that those who are its guardians are defiling it with those things from which Allah commanded to keep it pure and clean, it is the common responsibility of the Muslims of the entire world to hold the hands of the guardians. Its guardians cannot say that this is their internal affair, and others have no right to interfere in it.

Similarly, if, God forbid, some external power should attack it, Jihad for its protection and defense will become obligatory upon the Muslims of the entire world. In this matter, no national or international treaty and interest will be tolerated. If the government of some country stops from participating in this Jihad, even though it be a government of nominal Muslims, it will be obligatory upon the believers to fight against it too, because the responsibility for the protection and defense of the Sanctuary is not upon the people of Makkah or their government alone but upon every believer; under no circumstances can this be declared an external dispute. In the rights and responsibilities of the Sanctuary, every Muslim is an equal partner—the Sanctuary is the heart of the Muslim Ummah. Its reform is the reform of the entire Ummah, and its corruption is the corruption of the entire nation.

From the words "sawa'an al-'akifu feehi wal-badi", these results emerge obviously, as mentioned above, but a group of our jurists has also extracted this issue from it: in the limits of the Sanctuary, neither can any house be anyone's personal property, nor can he receive rent of any house from anyone else. In my view, this extraction is weak. If you look at the words of the verse, you will realize that the words "ja'alnahu linnasi sawa'an al-'akifu feehi wal-badi" have entered as an attribute of the Sacred Mosque, not as an attribute of the entire Makkah or all limits of the Sanctuary. For this reason, in this issue, I prefer the school of the Shafi'is who are not in favor of this restriction.

"Wa man yurid feehi bi-ilhadin bidulmin nudhiqhu min 'adhabin aleemin"—after "yurid", the preposition "Bi" is a clue that here this word contains the meaning of "humma" (he intended). The explanation of this style has passed in other places. "Ilhad" means deviation, crookedness, and impiety. "Bidulmin" is a substitute (badal) for "bi-ilhadin", and this word is well-known in the Holy Quran for the meaning of Shirk.

The purpose of the verse is: this house is such a house that if anyone commits any impiety in it, and that too an impiety like Shirk, We will make him taste a painful punishment. It should be kept in mind that "iradah" and "hamm" have a minor difference: "iradah" manifests with thought, but "hamm", as entered in "wa hammoo bimalam yanaloo" [At-Tawbah: 74] and "wa laqad hammat bihi wa hamma bha" [Yusuf: 24], manifests also from some temporary inclination of the soul. The meaning is: the conditions and manners of this house are very strict. Here, grasp is also on those wrong things which manifest from temporary inclinations of the soul.

"Be alert, because the path is on the edge of the sword for the step!"

By adding "dulm" after "ilhad", this reality is clarified: when common crookedness here is subject to grasp, how can any room be found here for that great Shirk whose vast business the Quraysh have spread here!

The true history of Hazrat Ibrahim and the House of Allah:
Now, the history of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and the House of Allah is being mentioned so that it may be clearly clarified to the Quraysh that all those purposes for which Allah had built this house through Hazrat Ibrahim and Hazrat Ismail (peace be upon them), they have ruined all of them; for this reason, they are worthy of being expelled from here, and those who fulfill the right of this great divine trust should be made responsible for it.

"Wa idh bawwa'na li-Ibraheema makana al-bayti"—"tabwi'ah" means to shelter, settle, and populate. That is: "Remember that event of history when, by Our special command, after Ibrahim's migration, We settled him and Ismail in this place where the House of Allah is." After "bawwa'na", "Li" is a clue that this place was not established as the dwelling of Hazrat Ibrahim alone, but the dwelling of his offspring (Hazrat Ismail) was also established as this. The Arabs were acquainted with their history in brief and took pride in being the offspring of Hazrat Ibrahim and Ismail (peace be upon them) and the heirs of their creed, but the Jews made this conspiracy, long before the Prophet's mission, to remove from the Torah all those things or alter them which testified to Hazrat Ibrahim's connection with the House of Allah, so that the mission of the final messenger might not be proved among the children of Ismail. They made a self-crafted history of Hazrat Ibrahim and tried to show that Hazrat Ibrahim, after the migration, made his center in Syria, not in Makkah, and the son whom he sacrificed was Hazrat Isaac, not Hazrat Ismail, and then tried to smear black over Makkah, the House of Allah, and all things related to them. But how far could they succeed in hiding a reality brighter than the sun? Even now in the Torah, despite the Jews, such testimonies are present which are sufficient to expose their conspiracy. The teacher-imam has exposed all these conspiracies of the Jews in his work 'Ar-Ra'y as-Saheeh fee man huwa adh-Dhabeeh', and we too are addressing all necessary discussions in this book. Take a look at what we have written under verses 132-141 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

The mirror of history before the Quraysh:
"An la tushrik bee shay'an wa tahhir baytiya lit-ta'ifeena wal-qa'imeena war-rukka'i as-sujood"—this same theme, in almost these words, has entered in Surah Al-Baqarah:
(And We charged Abraham and Ishmael, [saying], "Purify My House for those who perform Tawaf and those who are staying [there] for worship and those who bow and prostrate.") [Al-Baqarah: 125]

We have pointed out above: this verse is a mirror so that the Quraysh may look at their faces in it, seeing with what purpose and with what command and direction Allah had appointed Hazrat Ibrahim for the construction of this house, and now into what the Quraysh have turned it, yet they are sitting as its monopolists and stopping those who want to renew the original purposes of this house.

"Rijal" is the plural of "rajil", which means those who walk on foot. "Damir" refers to that camel or horse which has become lean due to the length of the journey; this word is used equally for both masculine and feminine. The word "kull" (every) with it is for emphasis of the attribute, as we say: "huwa al-'alimu kullu al-'alimi" (he is extremely learned). "Fajj" means the path between mountains, and "'ameeq" is its attribute. Since mountain paths are narrow, they become deep due to the abundance of movement.

Hazrat Ibrahim... public proclamation:
That is, one direction Hazrat Ibrahim received was that mentioned in the above verse; the second direction was to make a public proclamation for the Hajj of this house, so that people from far and near might come to benefit from this spring of good and blessing. In this too, there is a sarcasm on the attitude of the Quraysh: they were supposed to invite all creation for the Hajj and visitation of this house, but they did completely the opposite—they are sitting as guardians over it, and when the true followers of the creed of Ibrahim intend this house, they hiss at them and bite them.

Glad tidings for Hazrat Ibrahim:
"Ya'tooka rijalan..." until the end of the verse. This is the reply to the command; for this reason, this is glad tidings for Hazrat Ibrahim: that when you make the proclamation of Hajj, people will come to this house on foot and on mounts, passionately. People's mounts will become lean due to the length of the journey, and the paths of Makkah will become deep due to the abundance of movement.

Restrictions on Hajj are unlawful:
From these metaphors, the abundance, desire, passion, and ecstasy of those coming is expressed, whose testimony even today, after thousands of years, is given by the roads of Makkah and Madinah and the fields of Mina and Arafat on the occasion of Hajj, and every observer can estimate how the glad tidings given to Hazrat Ibrahim manifested, and how with the lapse of time, it is constantly increasing. Formerly in this path of love, only camels became lean; now the progress of the times has included buses, lorries, cars, and airplanes in this race. The context of these verses is proof: the pleasure and approval of Allah is only that the desire and passion of His servants for the visitation of His house should increase day by day. For this reason, the restrictions many governments, and those too calling themselves Muslim governments, have imposed on Hajj under different names—visas, quotas, lots, and foreign exchange—are not only completely unlawful, but in our view, are included in "sadd 'an al-masjid al-haram" (stopping from the Sacred Mosque). Even if a non-Muslim government imposes any such restriction on the Muslims of its region, it is not authorized to do so; rather, forward from the verses, it will be clear that this thing is included in that persecution (fitnah) to eradicate which Jihad must be waged against that non-Muslim government by the Muslims, if they possess the capability to do so.

The worldly blessings of Hajj:
"Liyashhadoo manafi'a lahum"—in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, we have explained in detail: the months of Hajj served as a spring season for the commercial activities of the Arabs too. In this period, all things of the Quraysh's need reached the markets of Makkah from outside, and the goods of Makkah departed for foreign markets. If along with the actual purpose of Hajj, such lawful worldly benefits are obtained, this is not contrary to the temperament of that upright religion taught by Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him); rather, this is a testimony: this religion of nature is pure from every trace of monasticism. To the extent that even in its worship in which apparently the greatest manifestation of leaving the world is there, it has maintained the balance of combining religion and world. Only the true balance must be maintained: that Hajj does not remain merely a commercial or political journey.

"Wa yadhkuroo isma Allahi fee ayyamin ma'loomatin 'ala ma razaqahum min baheemati al-an'ami"...

Research on "baheemat al-an'am" has passed under verse 1 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

Meaning of "Ayyam ma'loomat":
"Ayyam ma'loomat" refers to the determined days of sacrifice. We have pointed in another place to the wisdom of religion: just as in the works of this world, times, seasons, and periods are considered, and if this is not maintained, the entire struggle goes in vain, similarly in worship too, place, times, days, and months are considered. If their care is not maintained as far as possible, the worship remains deprived of its original blessing. The days of sacrifice are prescribed by Allah and His Messenger, and these are a memorial of Hazrat Ibrahim's sacrifice. In them, keeping in view the ease of the Ummah, Allah and His Messenger have kept a limit of width too. Benefiting from this width is not contrary to piety, but considering these days is necessary in religion, and this is completely in accordance with the nature of this universe.

The lock of God's name on the lives of beasts:
"Wa yadhkuroo isma Allahi"—in this verse too and forward in the verses, slaughter and sacrifice are expressed as mentioning the name of Allah. The reason for this special care is: in the ignorance, all sacrifices of the polytheists were for their idols. For the complete eradication of this trial, Allah placed the lock of His name on the lives of all beasts, which can be opened only by the key of His name. Without this, taking the life of any beast was made unlawful in religion. "'Ala ma razaqahum min baheemati al-an'ami" is the argument: why is it necessary to slaughter beasts only in the name of God? This is necessary because they are granted by God alone; for this reason, slaughtering them in the name of anyone other than God is both ungratefulness to God and humiliation of the animals. In this style, there is also encouragement and motivation for gratitude to Allah. That is: Allah has granted beasts to His servants out of His favor and mercy; their right is that the servants should present their offering in the presence of their Lord as gratitude for this blessing.

Some conceptions of the polytheists:
"Fakuloo minha wa at'imoo al-ba'isa al-faqeera"—it is stated: "This sacrifice you are directed to present is not because God derives any benefit from these sacrifices. Neither the flesh of these sacrifices nor their blood reaches God. This presented offering of yours is returned to you—you eat of it yourselves and feed the hungry and needy." The example of sacrifice is exactly that someone places his crown at the feet of the king, and the king, honoring this crown at his feet, then places it back on his head... It should be kept in mind here: polytheistic nations, without exception, believe that their deities benefit and enjoy their presented sacrifices. Let alone the polytheists, even among the Jews, this conception is found that the Lord enjoys the sweet savor of some sacrifices. The Quran has put an end to these conceptions in this verse and forward in the verses.

The final rituals of Hajj:
"Tafath" means dirt and dust. "Taqda tafathahu" means he removed his dirt and dust. This is a pointer to the performance of the final rituals of Hajj after being free of sacrifice. Due to the restrictions of Ihram and the activities of rituals, hair, etc., also grow, and the dirt of the body also increases. God likes this state of disheveled hair and dusty body to be upon the servant in these days, but after the Hajj, he should trim his hair, bathe, change clothes, and if he has vowed some sacrifice, fulfill it, and perform the final circumambulation of the House of Allah to be free of Hajj.

Bait al-'Ateeq:
The House of Allah is expressed here as "Bait al-'Ateeq". "'Ateeq" means original and ancient. The reason for calling the House of Allah 'Ateeq, as we have explained under Al-Baqarah 125, is: the original and ancient house of God built by Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) is this, not the Temple of Jerusalem as the Jews claim. The Temple of Jerusalem was built long after Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), by the hands of Hazrat Sulayman (peace be upon him); then, its construction too was such that the direction of its actual altar was toward the House of Allah, because the original Qiblah of all offspring of Ibrahim was the House of Allah. Although the Jews tried to put a veil over all these things, the testimonies of the actual reality are present in the Torah even today, and in the commentary of Al-Baqarah, we have explained them.

"Dhalika" standing for the entire sentence:
When "dhalika" (that) comes in this manner, it stands for the entire sentence. That is: "These are the matters which were told to Ibrahim regarding the House of Allah, its Hajj, and its rituals. Hear and understand them well." This is, as it were, a word of warning. The directions given to Hazrat Ibrahim were completed on this sentence. Now forward, directions are being given to those who claimed the inheritance of the House of Allah, but were completely unaware of the responsibilities of this trust.

Direction to respect all sacred things of Allah:
"Wa man yu'addim hurumati Allahi fahuwa khayrun lahu 'inda rabbihi"—by "hurumat Allah" (the sacred things of Allah) is meant all those things whose respect Allah has commanded—for example, the Sanctuary, the Sacred Mosque, the offering, and the collars, etc. The respect of all these things has been coming down from the time of Hazrat Ibrahim. The reason for emphasizing the respect and honor of these sacred things here is: the polytheists had completely changed all the sacred traditions of Hazrat Ibrahim for their worldly interests. For example, take the four sacred months. By inventing the practice of Nasi', as detailed before, they adjusted them according to the solar months instead of the lunar, so that they could make them compatible from a commercial point of view.
