In this verse, attention is directed to the reality: due to the restrictions of respecting the sacred things of Allah, it is possible that apparently some worldly interests suffer some loss. But the thing to be looked at is: these are not temporary benefits but are their eternal blessings of the Hereafter. Those who maintain their respect under all circumstances for the sake of these eternal blessings should remember: in the sight of Allah, this thing will be a cause of very great good for them.

Refutation of the self-carved decrees of the polytheists:
"Wa uhillat lakumu al-an'amu illa ma yutla 'alaykum fajtaniboo ar-rijsa mina al-awthani wajtaniboo qawla az-zoori"—the above segment, as we have pointed out, is by way of warning. Now, in connection with "'ala ma razaqahum min baheemati al-an'ami", it is clarified: the decrees the polytheists have issued regarding various beasts, based merely on their polytheistic delusions—that such beast is lawful, such is unlawful, such is permissible for men, such is unlawful for women, riding such type of beast is permissible, and riding such is not permissible, etc., etc.—all these things are merely self-carved. In the creed of Ibrahim, they have no basis. In the creed of Ibrahim, unlawful are only those beasts which are being recited to you in the Quran. This refers to those details which have passed in Surah Al-An'am. See verses 146-153 of Surah Al-An'am. All this prohibition and lawfulness, since it was based on polytheistic beliefs and superstitions, it is said: "Avoid that filth which you have loaded upon yourselves in connection with your idols. 'Wajtaniboo qawla az-zoori'—and avoid falsehood against God." That is: "Prohibition and lawfulness you do of your own accord, but to give it a religious sanctity, you attribute it to God, claiming He commanded you these things. This is a severe lie and fabrication against Allah, glorified be His name; avoid it." In Surah Al-An'am, this thing is expressed as "iftera'an 'ala Allah" (fabrication against Allah): (They prohibited what Allah had provided them, fabricating against Allah.) [Al-An'am: 140]

Meaning: "The honor of all signs of Allah and the performance of all rituals are desired in such a manner that every action be done turning to Him single-mindedly, remaining completely pure from every trace of Shirk. If any trace of Shirk is mixed in any action, it is completely void in the sight of Allah."

Representation of the polytheist:
"Wa man yushrik billahi..." until the end of the verse. This is the representation of the polytheist, showing that by committing Shirk, man cuts off from his original center, and when he cuts off from his original center, nothing can be said as to which devil's hands he may fall into. He is like a ship without an anchor which can collide with any rock, and like a cut-off kite which the wind can throw wherever it wishes. From the honor man obtains through Monotheism, as soon as he deprives himself of it, he falls into the same level of depth. It is said: "He who associates partners with Allah, his example is as if he fell from the sky, and the birds snatched him, or the wind threw him into some deep abyss!"

This clarifies: by committing Shirk, man does not harm God in the least, but deprives himself of that honor, security, and protection which Allah granted him through Monotheism. Then, he is the target of every trial of Satan and the victim of every trap laid by him.

The actual condition for the honor of signs:
This "dhalika" too is of the same kind as passed in verse 30 above—meaning, hear well those things told above and grasp them, and furthermore, remember well: the signs Allah has prescribed—their respect cannot be fulfilled merely by apparent show; rather, for it, the piety of the heart is required. Signs, as we have explained under Al-Baqarah 158 "Inna as-safa wal-marwata min sha'a'iri Allahi", have been prescribed as the indicator and manifestation of some great reality. The actual purpose of them is the remembrance of those realities which are implied in them. If the care of those realities is not maintained, the right of the honor of signs is not fulfilled merely by apparent customs. Their correct respect is fulfilled only when that effect is acquired from them for whose creation they have been prescribed. Forward regarding sacrifice, which is a great sign, this reality is clarified:
(Their meat will not reach Allah, nor will their blood, but what reaches Him is piety from you.) [Al-Hajj: 37]

Regarding sacrifice, it is known that this is a memorial of Hazrat Ibrahim's sacrifice. He, by sacrificing his beloved son—Hazrat Ismail—demonstrated the reality of Islam: that a Muslim must be ready at all times to sacrifice his dearest thing for the sake of his Lord. If this reality of sacrifice is not present, and a man runs a knife on the neck of an animal, although apparently he made a sacrifice, in reality, he remained unaware of the spirit of this great sign, whereas the thing that reaches God is that spirit alone, not the meat or blood of his sacrifice.

On the word "mahill" (place of offering/slaughter), discussion has passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, after it, the preposition "Ila" is a clue that some word is assumed as omitted from which the meaning is born: "then their place of slaughter is at the Ancient House."

Reform of some innovations of the polytheists:
When the polytheists nominated some beast for offering, they considered any kind of benefit from it unlawful. The Quran reformed them: for the respect of these signs, this thing is not necessary. Nurture your animals of sacrifice and benefit from them until the time of their sacrifice arrives. By this benefit, no difference occurs in their sanctity, but it is necessary that when the time of their sacrifice arrives, bring them to the Ancient House of Allah and sacrifice them for Allah alone. If they are sacrificed on some other altar or temple in the name of anyone other than God, their sanctity is ruined. The explanation of "Bait al-'Ateeq" has passed above. "Thumma" (then) here, in my view, represents the order; for this reason, I consider this benefit permissible until the sacrifice is executed.

Sacrifice is an ancient worship:
On the word "mansak", research has passed under Al-Baqarah 200. Among its different meanings, sacrifice is meant here. The meaning is: in the divine laws, sacrifice is a highly ancient worship. Allah started this method of worship for the Ummah of every prophet, so that the servants of Allah might fulfill the right of His gratitude on the beasts granted by Him in this manner. For the ancient nature of sacrifice as a worship, this is sufficient testimony that in both the Torah and the Quran, the mention of Hazrat Adam's son Abel's sacrifice is present.

"Liyadhkuroo isma Allahi 'ala ma razaqahum min baheemati al-an'ami"—this characteristic of this worship started by God is described: Allah prescribed this worship specifically for His gratitude, so that His servants might present their offerings in His presence for His pleasure, and thus express gratitude for the blessing granted by Him. The meaning is: in all divine religions, the prescription of sacrifice and its actual spirit has been this. If anyone has associated others in it, this is a misguidance created by innovators. It has no connection with the religion of God.

"Fa-ilahukum ilahun wahidun falahu aslimoo"—the meaning is: "The Deity of all of you is One, so do not associate any other in His sacrifice and worship; rather, with complete single-mindedness, hand yourselves over to Him. This handing over is the actual spirit of sacrifice."

The reality of "Ikhbat":
"Wa bashshiri al-mukhbiteen"—"khabt" means low and depressed land. From this comes "ikhbat", which means expression of humility, self-abasement, and modesty. Here, by this word, that reality is expressed which was directed by the word "aslimoo" (submit). The actual spirit of Islam is "ikhbat"—meaning, not only the apparent but also the heart of man should bow down before his Lord. Those who possess this "ikhbat" are the true believers and Muslims, and for them alone is the glad tiding of God's pleasure and His heaven.

This is the attribute of the "mukhbiteen": when the name of Allah is mentioned before them and His verses are recited to them, they do not turn their faces like the arrogant, whose mention will come forward in verse 72 of this Surah; rather, their hearts tremble with the fear of God. "Was-sabireena 'ala ma asabahum"—and whose state is: they are bearing every calamity in God's path with complete courage; they are not like those whose state is described under the verse "Wa mina an-nasi man ya'budu Allaha 'ala harfin", who became claimants of faith but are not ready to suffer any blow or bear any risk in this path. "Wal-muqeemee as-salati"—this is a structure of genitive. On the mutual connection of patience and prayer, we have discussed in more than one place. Its meaning is not merely that they perform prayer, but that they are constantly maintaining the care of prayer. "Wa mimma razaqnahum yunfiqoon"—and they spend of what We have provided them. Keep in view the aspect of ease and encouragement in this style of spending, and do not let the connection between prayer and spending go out of sight. To understand the wisdom of religion, it is necessary to understand the mutual connection of these parts.

Meaning of "Budn":
"Budn" is the plural of "badanah". This word is used for camels, but here it is used specifically for those camels which have been nominated for sacrifice, and whose status has become of "Hady" (offering) and "Qala'id" (collars). The reason for this specificity is: in their definition, it is said: "ja'alnahaha lakum min sha'a'iri Allahi" (We have made them for you among the signs of Allah). It is obvious that the status of being a sign is not possessed by every camel, but is possessed only by the camels of offering.

A specific reason for mentioning camels:
Above in verse 28, those common beasts whose sacrifice is prescribed have been mentioned. After that, there was no great need to mention camels, because they are included in "baheemat al-an'am", but for a specific reason, they are mentioned with special care. That is: the camel is among the beloved beasts of the Arabs, but the Jews, on the basis of a weak narration of theirs, whose mention has passed under Al-Imran 93, had declared it unlawful. Taking shelter under this narration of theirs, they made the issue of camel sacrifice also a medium for mischief-making and whispering against Islam. They began to spread this propaganda: "How can this new religion be the religion of Hazrat Ibrahim, when it has made the sacrifice of an animal declared unlawful by all prophets a medium of closeness to God!" The Quran has refuted this delusion of the Jews in verse 93 of Surah Al-Imran, and demanded from the Jews: if they have any proof that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had declared the camel unlawful, let them present it.

"Lakum feeha khayrun" (for you in them is good)—this is the reason for declaring the camel sacrifice among the signs of Allah: that inside it are great blessings and great benefits for you. Under the verse "Lan tanaloo al-birra" [Al-Imran: 92], we have submitted: the sacrifice of that animal is liked by Allah which is dear and beloved. The camel is among the most beloved animals of the Arabs. This was the ship of their desert, the companion of all their travel and stay, and the sole medium of all their commercial activities. They derived maximum benefits from its milk, meat, and skin. The Quran, because of these very benefits and blessings of it, has directed the attention of the Arabs repeatedly to this blessing of Allah. It is obvious that what is so beneficial and blessed for the Arabs in terms of the world, if they sacrifice it for the pleasure of their Lord, this can be a very great medium of closeness to God for them.

"Fadhkuroo isma Allahi 'alayha sawaffa fa-idha wajabat junoobuha fakuloo minha wa at'imoo al-qani'a wal-mu'tarra"...

This showed the method of their sacrifice: stand them in rows facing the Qiblah, and then slaughter them. The reason for expressing slaughter as mentioning the name of Allah we have explained above. This standing of them in rows is, as it were, their standing for prayer in the presence of God, and when they fall on their sides after slaughter, this is, as it were, their prostration in the presence of the Lord. It is said: "After that, benefit from its meat yourselves, and feed the self-respecting needy and the beggars." From some narrations, it is known that the polytheists considered benefiting from their sacrifices unlawful. Islam reformed this innovation too.

Here, for the needy, the words "qani'" and "mu'tarr" are used. The actual needy, as passed in verse 28 above, are the poor and destitute, but among the poor, there are many whose self-respect does not tolerate the shame of begging, and there are many who beg. For the first type of needy, the word "qani'" is used, and for the second type, "mu'tarr". "Mu'tarr" means one who exposes himself for begging. In the verse, the advancement of "qani'" shows: their right is prior, and since they do not stretch their hands before anyone, it is the duty of the givers to reach them, not expecting them to reach the givers. Under the verse "La yas'aloona an-nasa ilhafa" [Al-Baqarah: 273], we have written on this issue in detail, pointing out: in the sight of Allah, the preferred attitude for the poor is of self-respect, although in state of necessity, begging is also permitted.

"Kadhalika sakharnahaha lakum la'allakum tashkuroon"—the meaning is: "This is merely the favor and mercy of Allah that He has handed over the rein of a great animal like the camel to your hand in this manner, that you may benefit from it as you wish in your travel and stay, and slaughter it when you wish. It cannot deviate by a hair's breadth from your obedience. If God did not wish, let alone the camel, you could not make even a small animal subject to your command in this manner." The natural and cosmic right of this favor of God is: remain grateful to Him, fulfill His rights, and do not associate others in these rights.

Reminder of the actual reality:
The meaning is: God does not enjoy the meat or blood of sacrifices, as the polytheists have assumed, but is pleased with that piety, Islam, and "ikhbat" which is born inside the presenters from these sacrifices. So, while presenting these sacrifices, create this spirit of piety inside yourselves. If this thing is not born, this is merely shedding the blood of an animal—there is no product of it.

"Kadhalika sakhara-ha lakum litukabbiroo Allaha 'ala ma hadakum"—this is the repetition of the same theme in other words which passed in "Kadhalika sakharnahaha..." above. However, in this, the method of that gratitude is shown: that it be in the form of glorification and praise of God—meaning, while presenting this offering in the court of God, only the honor and grandeur of God be confessed and proclaimed, and no other than God be associated in it. Consequently, in compliance with this command, these beasts are turned toward the Qiblah, glorification is recited over them, and the confession of "minka wa laka" (from You and for You) is made. Furthermore, in this verse, the actual motive of this gratitude is also pointed out: this gratitude is for God having guided you—meaning, guided you to this Islam and "ikhbat" which Hazrat Ibrahim demonstrated by sacrificing his only son.

"Wa bashshiri al-muhsineen"—the meaning of "muhsin" (well-doer) we have explained in more than one place: it refers to those good-doing people who perform every command of God keeping Him present and seeing, in the manner it must be performed. Here, based on context, this theme is omitted: those who present their sacrifice keeping these conditions in view are in reality the good-doers. Give the glad tidings of God's pleasure and His heaven to such good-doers!

The upcoming theme... Verses 38-41:
These verses were sent down in Madinah, and since they are, as we have pointed out in the introduction, branched from and serve as explanation of those matters mentioned above, they obtained their place here in the arrangement of the Codex.

Take a look back at verse 25. There, pointing to the Quraysh, it is said: those who are stopping Muslims from the path of Allah and the Sacred Mosque, while no monopoly can be established over the Sacred Mosque, are great wrongdoers, and Allah will make such wrongdoers taste a painful punishment. After that, in the light of the history of the House of Allah and its rituals and signs, it is shown that the Quraysh have ruined all the sanctity of this house; they have no right to remain dominant over this house.

Within these verses, obviously, this pointer was implied: the true heirs and custodians of this house are the Muslims, not the Quraysh. But in Makkah, the Muslims were completely helpless and forced; for this reason, they could not take any practical step to acquire this right of theirs. After migrating to Madinah, when they took the form of an organization, this feeling naturally arose in them with intensity: they must not remain deprived of the blessings of this house. But what would be its form? It is obvious: when the Quraysh had expelled them from this house with such enmity, they could not easily tolerate letting Muslims perform Hajj and Umrah. If Muslims tried for it, war would inevitably occur, and that too in the limits of the Sanctuary and the sacred months, which has been considered detestable in both ignorance and Islam. The Quraysh and their allies—the People of the Book—would make this thing also a medium of propaganda against Islam and Muslims, saying: "Look, these people of the new religion have attacked the sanctity of the Sanctuary and the sacred months too, whose audacity was never committed by anyone before them." All these questions were before the Muslims at that time. In these verses, those questions are answered.

Glad tidings for the oppressed Muslims:
From the very introduction of this discussion, Allah has raised the glad tidings for the oppressed Muslims. He said: "The oppression the Quraysh have committed against Our believing servants, We will defend Our oppressed servants against it." That is, now the time has come for the Muslims to rise for the protection and defense of their rights; God will be with them in this Jihad and will help them at every step.

Declaration of detachment from the Quraysh:
"Inna Allaha la yuhibbu kulla khawwanin kafoorin"—"kull" here is for emphasis of the attribute, and by "khawwan kafoor" (treacherous ungrateful one), pointer is made to the disbelievers of Quraysh. "Khawwan" means treacherous, betrayer, and covenant-breaker. This is a pointer to the fact: they have violated all those covenants and conditions under which the guardianship of the Sanctuary was entrusted to them. Every single thing of those purposes for which Hazrat Ibrahim had built this house, and of those responsibilities he had placed on his offspring regarding this house, they have desecrated. For this reason, they are not worthy of the guardianship of this house, and such betrayers and traitors are never liked by God.

Along with "khawwan", the attribute of "kafoor" (ungrateful) is to describe their ungratefulness and ingratitude. In the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, we have explained in detail: the high position of religious leadership and political power the Quraysh obtained in the entire Arabia was obtained entirely by the grace of Allah, but instead of appreciating this blessing and remaining grateful to their Lord, they established their monopoly over this house, receiving all rights in its name, but not only did they completely forget its rights and duties but also, through completely opposite actions, threw all its respect into the dust. It is said: "What connection can God have with such betrayers and ungrateful ones! Allah never likes such betrayers and ungrateful ones." By "la yuhibb" (He does not like) is meant: God holds such covenant-breakers detestable. The respite they were to receive, they have received. Now the time has come for God to purify His Sanctuary from these impure ones and entrust its guardianship to those servants of His who fulfill the right of this Abrahamic trust.

Permission of Jihad for Muslims in their defense:
The object of "idhina" (permission is given) is omitted here based on context. That is: "Now, permission of war is given to those Muslims with whom war is waged." Until this time, since the Muslims had no regular organizational form, despite being the target of every kind of oppression, they were not given the permission of war; instead, patience was directed. This was because the war of scattered individuals cannot succeed in eradicating corruption and establishing truth and justice, and in Islam, war is permissible only for establishing truth and justice. But after migrating to Madinah, the situations changed; for this reason, they were given the permission to raise weapons in their defense. Additionally, this permission was also necessary from this aspect: the matter was of the Sanctuary and the limits of the Sanctuary. Muslims could not take any action regarding the Sanctuary until they received permission for it from Allah and His Messenger. It should be kept in mind: only defensive war is mentioned here, because until this stage, the Muslims did not possess the strength to bear more weight than this. As for the question: is offensive war also permissible for Muslims? For the answer to this, read our commentary of Surah Al-Bara'at (At-Tawbah) with contemplation.

"Bi-annahum dulimoo" (because they have been wronged)—this is another reason in favor of this permission. That is: first, the defense of oneself is the right of every person and every community; then, additionally, this is also the case: these Muslims have been wronged. The detail of this oppression is coming in the subsequent verse.

"Wa inna Allaha 'ala nasrihim laqadeerun"—this is the confirmation of the above-mentioned promise of defense from Allah and also a kind of threat for the disbelievers of Quraysh—meaning, let no one assume: what is the status of these handful of Muslims, and who can strengthen them by helping them? Allah is fully powerful over helping them, and when He has stood up to help them, no power of the world can defeat them.

Explanation of the oppression upon Muslims:
"Alladhina ukhrijoo min diyarihim bighayri haqqin"—this is the explanation of that oppression upon Muslims pointed to in the above verse. That is: "These people have been expelled from their homes without any crime." In this style of expression, all those oppressions and distresses in which Muslims were involved have come spontaneously. This is because no one is ready to abandon his homeland and his home unless the land of his homeland is made narrow for him.

"Illa an yaqooloo rabbuna Allahu" (except that they say, "Our Lord is Allah")—this is a highly eloquent phrase. Its meaning is: "The crime of these oppressed ones, if any exists, is that they say that our Lord is Allah alone!" That is, their greatest virtue and their invite to the greatest right has become their greatest crime, and they have been so constrained in this crime that these poor ones had to bid farewell to their homes.

Some have understood from this: the Quran is presenting the word of Monotheism as a highly harmless word—meaning, this is not something for anyone to be angry about, and the disbelievers of Quraysh unnecessarily became angry about it to pursue the Muslims. After all, what difference does it make if someone believes in only one God! Those who have understood so have merely a happy misunderstanding. The Quraysh were not so dull as not to understand the implications of this word. They knew where its blow fell; for this reason, they were its enemy. This is a separate matter: in this manner, they became the enemy of the greatest right. The Quran has directed attention to their very foolishness here, and in a highly subtle manner.

"Wa lawla daf'u Allahi an-nasa ba'dahum biba'din lahuddimat sawami'u wa biya'un wa salawatun wa masajidu yudhkaru feeha ismu Allahi katheeran"...

Meaning of "Sawami'":
"Sawami'" is the plural of "sawma'ah". Originally, this word came for those high mountains and places where Christian monks lived a life of solitude and isolation for worship. For this reason, if its translation is made as "monasteries", it will be appropriate.

Meaning of "Biya'":
"Biya'" is the plural of "bee'ah". This word comes for the temples of both Jews and Christians. But forward, another word has entered for the temples of the Jews. For this reason, the closest is: by this is meant the churches of the Christians. In their system of monasticism, both monasteries and churches held equal importance.

Meaning of "Salawat":
"Salawat" is the plural of "salat". This word comes for the synagogues of the Jews. In Hebrew, its origin is "salota".

The wisdom of the permission of Jihad:
Now, the wisdom is being mentioned: why has Allah given His believing servants the permission to raise the sword, to the extent that even in the Sanctuary and the limits of the Sanctuary, if they are attacked, they have the right to defend themselves and expect from their Lord that He will help them? It is said: "This permission is given because if Allah had not repelled the wicked and devils through His righteous servants, all monasteries, all churches, all synagogues, and all mosques in which the name of Allah is mentioned abundantly would have been demolished long ago." Consequently, Allah always directed His prophets and messengers and His righteous servants to wage Jihad, and they, through Jihad, protected all religious values and signs. Similarly today, Muslims are also given this permission to rise for the protection of Allah's Sanctuary and His signs, and if its current usurping betrayer claimant-custodians obstruct their path, they must answer them in the same coin.

Answer to the opponents of Jihad:
It should be kept in mind: realizing this courage of the Muslims, the Quraysh had already started propaganda against them: "Now these people of the new religion do not want to maintain even the sanctity of the Sanctuary and the limits of the Sanctuary," and the Jews and Christians too, joining their tune, were declaring this desire of Jihad of the Muslims contrary to piety. The Quran has answered these claimants of piety in other places too, and here too has answered all of them: if Jihad in God's path is contrary to piety, it means that the entire carpet of religion be rolled up, and mullahs of corruption be given a free hand to fulfill their desires to their heart's content.

The greatest opponents of Jihad were the Christians:
Here, this point should also be kept in mind: in the verse, first of all, as we have explained, the monasteries and churches of the Christians are mentioned. The reason for pointing to them first is: the greatest objectors to the Jihad of the Muslims, as will be known from the commentary of Surah Al-Hadid, were the Christians. Although there were some other motives too for this opposition of theirs, which will come in their place, their monastic conception had a great part in it. The Quran has struck a blow at their very conception here: those who, in the passion of opposing Islam, are declaring Jihad contrary to piety, must not ignore the reality: if this very conception of piety had been there before too, not a single corner of God's worship would have been protected on the earth today.

Regarding the temples of the Christians and Jews referenced here, remember: originally their status was the same as we have of mosques. In them, corruption was born only when the People of the Book fell into Shirk and innovation.

Some realities regarding the promise of help for the Muslims:
"Wa layansuranna Allahu man yansuruhu"—this has further confirmed and emphasized that promise of help, and along with it, pointed to some new realities too which are highly important.

One: those who rise for the protection of God's religion and His signs, in reality, rise for the help of their Lord. For this reason, it is necessary for God to help His servants who have risen for His help.

Second: the help of God always manifests for those who themselves rise to perform their duty in the path of truth. God does not help those who, like the children of Israel, wish for God to win the field for them and lay the throne of government, so that they may go to sit upon it.

Within "Inna Allaha laqawiyyun 'azeezun" (Indeed, Allah is powerful and exalted), there are also various aspects.

Some specific aspects of God being Powerful and Exalted:
God is Powerful and Exalted; for this reason, He is not in need of anyone's help. Those who help Him, in reality, open the path of God's help for themselves.

Muslims should not be frightened by their small number and the heavy assembly of the enemy; the Lord of Majesty who is promising to support them is Powerful and Exalted.

The disbelievers, seeing the current state of the Muslims, must not remain in this misunderstanding: what will these few chew! These very drops will now become a storm! Because they have the help and support of God, and God is Powerful and Exalted.

By "al-ard" (the land) here is meant the land of Makkah, as is the case in Ar-Ra'd 41 and Al-Anbiya 44; context is proof that here too it refers to the land of Makkah, because the Jihad permitted here, principally, is related to the freedom and purification of the land of the Sanctuary alone. It is said: "Its current usurping monopolist guardians have ruined all those purposes of this Sanctuary for which Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had settled his offspring here; however, if We grant power to Our servants in this land, through Our support and help, they will renew all its purposes. They will establish prayer, give zakah, enjoin what is right, and forbid what is wrong." Take a look at verses 124-128 of Surah Al-Baqarah. These are the exact purposes for which Allah had made this land the center of Hazrat Ibrahim and Ismail (peace be upon them), and the style of the verse in itself is showing that the Quraysh have ruined all these purposes.

The fruits of the power of Muslims:
The first glad tiding of the power and establishment of Muslims in the world is this, which begins from the land of the Sanctuary, whose status, as we have submitted above, is of the heart of the nation. On its reform or corruption depends the reform or corruption of the entire nation. This exact same duty is imposed on Muslims for every land where Allah grants them power. If they do not perform this duty, in the sight of God, just as the control of others is unlawful, the control of these nominal Muslims too is unlawful.

Prayer and Zakah are like the binding of the entire religion:
Prayer and Zakah, as we have explained in various places, are like the binding for the entire Shariah. If they are mentioned, as it were, the entire Shariah is mentioned.

Meaning of freedom of action on right and wrong:
On "ma'roof" (right) and "munkar" (wrong), detailed discussion has passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Some simpletons ask: when the Quran has permitted to act on "ma'roof", it means that the people of every group and every nation are free to act on their respective "ma'roof". These people should know: in the spheres of life in which God and His Messenger themselves have determined "ma'roof" and "munkar", no one has the right to make God's "ma'roof" "munkar" or His "munkar" "ma'roof". However, within the limits of the Shariah, the people of every nation and country are free to act on their "ma'roof".

Glad tidings to Muslims:
"Wa lillahi 'aqibatu al-umoori" (And to Allah belongs the outcome of [all] matters)—this is glad tidings for the Muslims: "Currently is the beginning of the work; for this reason, the situations are ambiguous and the end is in the veil. It is possible that to you and others, this glad tiding of power seems far-fetched, but the outcome of matters is in God's power alone. He will do what He wants to do."

The upcoming theme... Verses 42-57:
On verse 41, the explanatory verses which had come in the middle as parenthetical clauses were completed. Now forward, the speech is conjoined to its original connection, and in the light of the history of the prophets and their deniers, comfort is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions: "Remain assured, every promise of Allah will be fulfilled, and His every warning is impending. If some delay is occurring in its manifestation, it is occurring under the Divine Sunnah. In this manner, Allah tests His righteous servants and gives respite to their opponents to complete His proof upon them. If people possess eyes, the signs of this Divine Sunnah are present in this country at every step, but upon those whose hearts' eyes have become blind, how can these signs be effective!"

Within this, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is directed: "Remaining independent of the attitude of the opponents, like a clear warner, warn the people of the danger before them. If they place obstacles in the path of your call, this is not a novel thing. Every prophet and messenger faced such situations, but the word of Allah prevailed and all the feni-makings of the opponents and devils were destroyed by Allah. The opportunity Allah gave to the devils for these feni-makings is given because in this manner, the truth becomes fully clear and nikhars, and the people of knowledge and faith accept it with complete insight, then their steps do not falter..." In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Verses 42-44:
"Wa kuddhiba Moosa" (And Moses was denied)—the mention of Hazrat Musa's denial is made with the passive voice, whereas above, the denial of other prophets is mentioned conjoined to their respective nations. The reason for this can be: the denial of Hazrat Musa was committed not by his nation, but by the people of Pharaoh. Before the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the possessor of Shariah prophet was Hazrat Musa, with whose situations the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) could be most acquainted. For this reason, without any specification, as a concluding speech, the matter was said thus: "Denial indeed was committed before you of Moses too!"

Meaning of "Nakeer":
"Nakeer" is actually "nakeeree"; the 'ya' fell away, and the kasrah is its memorial. In Arabic, for the care of rhyme, etc., the 'ya' falls away in this manner. The meaning of "nakeer" is generally taken by lexicographers as denial, but I disagree with this. From the testimonies of Arabic speech, it is known that its meaning is not merely denial, but that denial with which intensity of jealousy, hatred, and aversion is found. For this reason, I have translated it as "phatkar" (cursing/rejection).

The common tradition of nations in the case of messengers:
In these verses, comfort is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "The treatment your nation is committing with you is not different in the least from what other nations did with their messengers. The history of the messengers and their nations is exactly this which is being repeated with you too, and with exact compatibility." The meaning is: "In this situation, neither does any shortcoming of yours have any part, nor is the reason for this that there was some lack in those things with which you were supposed to descend into the field. From your side, everything is fine, but the tradition of the nations is not to change. Just do your work! Leave the people to their state."

"Fa-amlaytu lil-kafireena thumma akhadhtahum fakayfa kana nakeer"—meaning, those nations who denied the messengers, Allah did not grasp them immediately, but gave them respite for a long period, but when this respite, instead of reform of state, increased their rebellion, Allah grasped them, and then grasped them in such a manner that He erased their existence. This same deal will be made with your nation too if it does not change its way.

Meaning of "Qasr Masheed":
"Qasr masheed" means a strong and high palace, and in accordance with the rules of Arabic, just as with "bi'r" (well) is the attribute "mu'attalah" (abandoned), similarly with it too, some attribute like "matrook" (abandoned) or "mahjoor" (deserted) or its synonym must be assumed, which is omitted based on contextual clarity.

The results of the moral decline of nations:
In the above verse, the reality described is testified to here through those signs of the land of Arabia over which the commercial caravans of the Arabs passed, and whose traditions, as clear from the speech of their poets and orators, were famous among them. It is said: "Have these people not seen how many cities and towns We destroyed because of their ungratefulness, and now their state is: their walls are fallen upon their roofs."

The destruction of nations comes as a result of their moral decline:
"Wa hiya dalimah" (while it was doing wrong)—this is in the place of state (hal), and the meaning of "zulm" (wrongdoing) we have been explaining: it refers to that wrong which nations commit against their own souls because of their ungratefulness and Shirk. This is a pointer to the reality: such destructions that come upon nations do not come merely as an accidental occurrence or the turn of times, as the fools have assumed, but their manifestation occurs from within their morals and character. They continued in ungratefulness to their Lord, and this wrong of theirs shook the joints of all their individual and collective morals, whose result ultimately was: first, the roofs of their greatness collapsed, then the foundations of their existence were also uprooted.

A picture of ruin:
"Fahiya khawiyatun 'ala 'urooshiha"—this is the picture of the situation. The collapse of great buildings begins from their roofs. After becoming abandoned, first their roofs become decayed and fall, then the walls too, due to becoming unprotected, collapse from wind and rain.

"Wa bi'rin mu'attalatin" is conjoined to "qaryatin". It should be kept in mind: in the land of Arabia, due to the scarcity of water, great importance was held by wells and springs. Towns settled only where water was available and wells could be made. Then, the maximum activity and chahal-pahal was also at the wells and springs; for this reason, the ruin of wells is a metaphor for the end of all gathering and activity.

With "wa qasrin masheedin" too, as we have pointed out, some attribute like "mu'attalah" must be assumed. For this reason, its meaning will be: "How many magnificent and high palaces are there which are completely abandoned, and before whose portals great chiefs and rulers once prostrated, now in their battlements and towers are the nests of crows!"

The blind of heart:
That is: "These lesson-giving signs We have left in the land only so that people may see them and obtain lesson from them, so that the capability of thinking inside their hearts and the capability of hearing inside their ears may awaken, because the true calamity of this world is not that the eyes on the heads become blind; rather, it is that the hearts in the chests become blind!" The meaning is: if the sight of the eyes of the head goes away, but the heart living in the chest is awake, a person can observe all realities despite being blind; but if the eyes of the heart are blind, he sees everything, but understands nothing!

Here, the restriction "fee as-sadoor" (in the breasts) with "quloob" (hearts) is a clue that with "al-absar" (the eyes), some word like "fee ar-ru'oos" (in the heads) or its synonym is assumed as omitted. The principle of opposition, whose examples have passed, demands this. Since the state of those without insight is being described here, it was necessary that the place of the heart be specified, showing: the true blindness is the blindness of the heart, and these deniers of the messenger are involved in this very blindness. Let no one consider them seeing on seeing the eyes of their heads open, because the light of insight inside the eyes comes through the path of the heart, and the eyes of their hearts are completely blind.

Evasion from obtaining lesson from signs:
This should be kept in mind here: the poets of the Arabs shed tears of blood on the signs of the beloved's abode—to the extent that they cried and made others cry on the signs of her stove and grinding stone—but the reach of all this grief of theirs was only to the signs of the beloved's abode. They did not try to obtain any lesson from these signs of history and the ruins of their country, which are telling thousands of stories of the heart's eye-opening through the language of the state. This same is the state of the archaeologists or researchers of our present times: they have filled museums with countless signs by making deep excavations, but their entire research is concentrated on this point: these signs belong to a civilization of five thousand years ago, or seven thousand years ago! That actual reality to which these signs are pointing, neither has entered anyone's understanding, nor perhaps will enter, whereas power has preserved these signs only for the remembrance of this reality, because insight inside hearts is born from this very remembrance of reality!

A delusion of man:
That is: "Those who deny you are making haste: 'Why does this punishment you are threatening us with not come?' They should know: it will surely come, God will not violate His promise. But man measures everything with his limited scales; for this reason, a period of some years or respite seems very long to him. He assumes: what is being said to him is all hoaxes, whereas these are not hoaxes but realities, but the days in God's presence are completely different from the days of this world. A day in God's presence, according to the calculation of the days of this world, is like a thousand years; for this reason, its seconds and minutes too exceed the period of years of this world. Man calculates everything according to his day of twenty-four hours, and God's programs are made according to His own calendar."

The scales of divine days:
The meaning is: do not try to measure and weigh the matters of God and His kingdom with your weights and scales. When centuries pass in your estimation, in God's presence, they are a matter of minutes and hours. This calculation of divine days mentioned here is also only for the approximation of understanding, like a parable, and the words "ka-alfi sanatin" (like a thousand years) themselves point to this. What is the actual reality of these days? It is known to God alone. This parable, as it has entered in the Quran, has entered in the Psalms and the Gospel too. The words of 2 Peter: 3 are:
"But, beloved, be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day."

The mention of the creation of heaven and earth in six days, as it is in the Quran, is in the Torah too, and the detail of the division of work Allah adopted in these six days is explained in Ha Mim As-Sajdah. It is obvious: by these, our days are not meant; rather, these are divine days whose length and breadth is known to God alone. For us, this brief knowledge is sufficient: God has made separate worlds, and the system of every world is separate. It is completely wrong to compare the matters of one to another. We must not try to measure the length and breadth of heaven and earth with our yardstick.

Those who have taken "yawm" (day) in the verse under discussion as the Day of Resurrection, and have established this length of it as a metaphor for the intensity of its punishment—in our view, their interpretation is contrary both to the context of speech and to the parallels of the Quran; particularly, attributing this statement to great commentators like Hazrat Ibn Abbas and Mujahid is completely contrary to reality. But after the explanation we have done above, there remains no need to refute it.

This is the answer to those making haste for punishment: "Do not be arrogant on God's respite. How many nations have passed whom We gave respite despite their ungratefulness and Shirk, but when they did not benefit from it, We ultimately grasped them, and then no one could deliver them from Us. The meaning is: this same end is going to be yours too if this arrogance of yours does not bend. 'Wa ilayhi al-maseer'—and also remember: ultimately the return of everyone is to Us alone. In the Hereafter, everyone will have to deal with Us alone. Neither anyone's helpers will be of any use to them there, nor their assumed partners and intercessors."

The actual responsibility of the messenger:
On "nadheerun mubeen" (a clear warner), we have discussed in another place. Among the Arabs, the practice was: where the tribe or caravan camped, a guard kept watch on some high place, and if he sensed any danger, he took off his clothes and stripped himself to announce the danger, after which all men of the tribe or caravan unsheathed their swords to be ready for defense. This was called "the naked warner" (an-nadheer al-'uryan). Since this expression was uncivilized, inappropriate for a prophet, the Quran made it civilized in the form of "nadheerun mubeen", but in it, the allusion is to the naked warner. The meaning is: "Just as a naked warner warns the tribe of danger, you, becoming a clear warner, warn the people of the coming time. But just warn them. Neither showing this danger is your responsibility, nor saving people from it is your responsibility. If people do not become warned even after this clear style of yours, the responsibility for the results is upon themselves." This theme emerges from the restriction in "innama" (only).

"Falladhina amanoo wa 'amiloo as-salihati lahum maghfiratun wa rizqun kareemun walladhina sa'aw fee ayatina mu'ajizeena ola'ika ashabu al-jaheemi"...

This is the actual warning of which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was directed to inform the people clearly. Although its beginning is with glad tidings for the believers, here this glad tiding too is the introduction of the warning, because the face of speech is toward the arrogant of Quraysh, and this glad tiding is a heavy blow to their pride. It is said: "Proclaim to the people: the time is coming when those of faith and righteous action will obtain the honor of forgiveness and noble provision, while those whose entire activity is in denying Our verses and trying to defeat Our messenger will be thrown into Hell."

"Rizqun kareem" (noble provision) is the fruit of forgiveness and a comprehensive expression for all blessings of heaven. Here, the attribute of "kareem" (noble) contains various aspects whose details will come in their place.

"Mu'ajazah" means that competition which is done to defeat someone. Here, by this is meant those activities of the disbelievers which they were doing to defeat the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).

The actual literal meaning of "tamanna", according to the specification of the author of 'Aqrab al-Mawarid', is this: "Among the people of Arabic, it is applied to the desire for the acquisition of a thing by way of love, and similarly to the speech that indicates this desire." I have given reference to this statement because in my view, in the light of Arabic speech, the correct meaning of this word is this. In the Quran, its different forms are used in seven or eight places. After that, from this same root, the word "umniyyah" has entered, in different forms of plural and singular, in seven or eight places. Everywhere, in every form, the actual spirit of the word is present—in our language, its meaning will be: to desire something, to wish, to yearn, to hope, or to make an appeal or attempt for some purpose. Similarly, "umniyyah" will mean desire, wish, yearn, hope, and appeal. I do not know from where some people have inserted the meaning of reading or recitation in this word—I have not found this word in this meaning anywhere in Arabic speech, while this is among the highly current words of the Arabic language. Some commentators have quoted a poem in support of this meaning, but first, to remove a highly current word from its well-known meaning, what is the weight of an unknown poem? Then, that poem too, in our view, is fabricated, and we can prove its fabrication, but what is the benefit of lengthening an unproductive discussion? This also holds no importance in our view: the author of 'Lisan' has also given reference to this meaning of the word. Despite admitting all the merits of the author of 'Lisan', we have pointed to this weakness of theirs in the introduction of the commentary: sometimes, under some word, they also quote the statements of the people of interpretation for support, and since this dictionary is of great status, those who are not among the critics of language take those statements; whereas these statements, as long as they are not supported by the usages of the language, hold no importance. In any case, in our view, there is not the slightest room for the meaning of reading or recitation in this word.

Now ponder over the meaning of the verse and its parts.

Regarding that "sa'y fee al-mu'ajazah" of the adversaries mentioned in the above verse, comfort is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "This is not a matter which has presented itself to you alone. Every messenger and prophet who came before you faced such situations. Whenever any prophet took a step, made a yearn, made a call, and tried to teach, guide, invite, and attract people for their guidance and reform, the wicked and corrupt placed obstacles in the path of his yearn in this manner, and tried to suppress his voice and defeat his call and message." It should be kept in mind here: whenever the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) stood up with the intention of calling and reform, Abu Lahab and Abu Jahl, etc., accompanied him like a shadow for his denial.

Here, the Prophet's step and action in the path of guidance and reform is expressed as "tamanna" (he yearned), and his effort and struggle as "umniyyah" (his yearn). By using these words, the purpose is to point to the reality: the yearn and desire of the Prophet, night and day, is: some path may open to enter God's guidance and His verses (ayat) into the hearts of people, and he continues to make various attempts to open this path, but the devils are always in wait to block this path, and wherever they see any word of the Prophet taking effect, they immediately start various kinds of propaganda against it. It should be remembered: the devils sleep a sweet sleep as long as they see that no voice of the word of truth is arising from any side, but when they sense that some call of truth has arisen, they see their future in danger, and then they rise for its opposition armed with all their weapons.

On the difference between prophet (nabi) and messenger (rasool), we have discussed in detail under Al-Imran 49. Here, by mentioning both words, the purpose is to express the reality: no prophet or messenger was saved from this trial. This trial is a Sunnah of Allah for the prophets and messengers, which has faced all of them.

The word "Satan" here is by way of genus, and by it is meant the devils of both jinn and humans. That is, both allied to make a united front to defeat the Prophet.

The feni-makings of the devils:
By "alqa ash-shaytanu fee umniyyatihi" is meant: they created obstacles in the Prophet's work, action, intentions, and plans in various ways, and tried to erase the effect of his word and message through various kinds of objections, gibes, and rumors.

"Fayansakhu Allahu ma yulqee ash-shaytanu thumma yuhkimu Allahu ayatihi"—"naskh" means, as explained under the verse of Naskh [Al-Baqarah: 106], to erase, and "ihkam" means to make firm and stable. The meaning is: the prophets and messengers faced this trial under the Sunnah of Allah, and its benefit, as will be explained forward, reached the call of truth. The result of this collision of truth and falsehood ultimately was: Allah laid down the trials raised by the devils like foam, and granted stability and firmness to His and His Messenger's words in the hearts.

"Wallahu 'aleemun hakeemun"—this is the confirmation of the theme of comfort through the reference to the attributes of Allah: "Allah makes this struggle happen between truth and falsehood, and the opportunity He gives to the devils for competition and feni-making against the call of His prophets is because all this is based on His knowledge and wisdom; for this reason, the Prophet must rely on Allah's knowledge and His wisdom, knowing that whatever presents itself is based on His knowledge and wisdom, and its result will come out better for his call."

This exact same theme, with different words, is stated in other places in the Quran. Some parallels: in Surah Al-An'am, it is said:
(And sismilarly We have made for every prophet an enemy—devils from among mankind and jinn, inspiring to one another decorative speech in delusion. But if your Lord had willed, they would not have done it, so leave them and that which they invent. And [it is] so the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter may incline to it and that they may be well pleased with it and that they may commit what they are committing.) [Al-An'am: 112-113]

From these verses, the complete explanation of the verse under discussion of Surah Al-Hajj is done. Particularly, the words "yoohan ba'duhum ila ba'din zukhrufa al-qawli ghuroora" (inspiring to one another decorative speech in delusion) must remain in sight. This is the exact same thing which is expressed as "alqa ash-shaytanu fee umniyyatihi" in the verse under discussion.

Similarly in Surah Al-Furqan, it is said:
(And similarly We have made for every prophet an enemy from among the criminals. But sufficient is your Lord as a guide and a helper. And those who disbelieved say, "Why was the Quran not revealed to him all at once?" Thus [it is] that We may strengthen thereby your heart. And We have spaced it distinctly. And they do not come to you with an argument but We bring you the truth and the best explanation.) [Al-Furqan: 31-33]

In this verse, an example of this satanic inspiration is also presented: when the Messenger of Allah presents the word of Allah to the people, these adversaries, to criticize the Messenger, raise this rumor: "If he is the Messenger of Allah, why does he not present this entire Quran all at once? After all, what is difficult for Allah!" The meaning is: when he is not doing so, it means (God forbid) he himself fabricates this discourse, and presents as much as he can fabricate, and attributes it to God falsely to establish his authority. 

The baseless narration of the satanic inspiration:
In the explanation of this verse, we have worked with such detail only so that no doubt may remain in anyone's mind. After this explanation, there remains no need to refute that useless narration which our commentators, may Allah forgive them, have recorded in their books as the cause of revelation (sha'n nuzool) of this verse. First, this verse, as you have seen, is not in need of any cause of revelation; rather, it is completely clear in its meaning and purpose and completely conjoined to its preceding and succeeding. Then, the calamity is: the narration they quote has no weight either in terms of narration (riwayah) or in terms of critical analysis (dirayah); rather, it is merely a satanic inspiration (alqa' shaytani) of the heretics (zanadiqah) which they fabricated to defile the infallibility of the prophets (peace be upon them), and our commentators, out of their simplicity, have been quoting it in their books.

Verses 53-54:
The first wisdom of giving respite to falsehood against truth:
Now, the wisdom is being clarified: why is this situation in this world—that whenever some call of truth and goodness arises, the wicked and devils also descend into the field with all their feni-makings? It is said: "This is because Allah has given respite to falsehood and the people of falsehood too in this world, so that whoever chooses the truth may choose it with choice and distinction, and whoever wants to go toward falsehood may go after the clarity of truth and the completion of proof. From this struggle, the faith of the believers becomes mature, and from this, the proof of Allah is completed upon the hypocrites and the hardhearted."

The hidden benefits of the trials of the wicked:
By "fitnah" is meant trial and examination. It is obvious: if the opportunity of competition and feni-making had not been given to falsehood and the people of falsehood against the truth, no distinction would have been made between the worshippers of truth and the worshippers of falsehood. On this opportunity being obtained, the people of falsehood, finding their preferred food, accompany the standard-bearers of falsehood; and those who are true worshippers of truth, despite all the falsehood-paintings of the people of falsehood, remain firm on the truth.

"Lilladhina fee quloobihim maradun wal-qasiyati quloobuhum"—by "marad" (disease) is meant hypocrisy, and by "qasiyat al-quloob" (hardhearted) is meant those hardcore adversaries of the Quraysh and the Jews by whose alliance this campaign against the call of truth was running. It is said: "Allah has given this respite to falsehood so that this may become a trial for these hypocrites and wicked ones, so that they may play whatever game they want to play against the truth and fulfill the yearns of their hearts."

"Wa inna ad-dalimeena lafee shiqaqin ba'eedin"—this is an expression of sorrow on the state of these wretched and shamat-struck ones: "Now they have gone so far in opposing the truth that there is no possibility of their return." In this is a pointer to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Now leave these people to their state; they have become the targets of the Divine Sunnah!"

The second wisdom of giving respite to falsehood against truth:
"Wa liya'lama alladhina ootoo al-'ilma annahu al-haqqu min rabbika fayu'minoo bihi fatukhbita lahu quloobuhum"—both "liya'lama" (that they may know) and "fayu'minoo" (so they may believe) are used in their complete meanings, and in "ootoo al-'ilma" (those who were given knowledge), by knowledge is meant the knowledge of the Book of Allah. This is the second wisdom of giving respite to falsehood: the other benefit of this is that in this manner, those who have become recipients of some knowledge of the Book become fully mature in their knowledge, and this reality becomes clearly illuminated upon them: the knowledge of the Book they received from you is completely truth and from their Lord. Then, this maturity of their knowledge grants complete stability and firmness to their faith too, and their hearts bow down to their Lord with complete single-mindedness and perfect pleasure.

Realities of things are clarified by their opposites:
It should be kept in mind here: the actual realities of things are clarified only by their opposites. You know and believe in a matter, but what can be said against it—if that has not come before you, there is a great possibility: when it comes, your knowledge and faith may become shaken. But if all that has come before you, and distinguishing between its pure and impure, you accepted this truth, you adopted it with complete insight. Then, there is no fear that any opposing wind can move you from your position. To create this insight in religion, Allah made this arrangement in this world: He gave the opponents of truth too this opportunity to spill whatever poison they want to spill against the truth, so that those who accept the truth do not adopt it merely by imitation, but adopt it with complete recognition.

It is obvious: those whose knowledge is mature, based on wisdom and insight, their faith too will not be imitative to be shaken by every blow; rather, from correct and mature knowledge, correct and mature faith is born, and from this mature faith, that "ikhbat" comes into existence which is the actual spirit of faith, and without which faith carries no weight in the scale of God.

Glad tidings of steadfastness for the believers:
"Wa inna Allaha lahadi alladhina amanoo ila siratin mustaqeemin"—just as regarding the hypocrites and the wicked, He said above: (And indeed, the wrongdoers are in extreme dissension—meaning, now these people have gone so far that there is no possibility of their return), similarly in this segment, He gave glad tidings to the believers: "Although the devils will try hard to block their path, Allah will not let their faith go in vain; rather, through His support, He will guide them toward the Straight Path." By "sirat mustaqeem" is meant that straight path which takes the servant to his Lord. In my view, the indefinite form here is to express its importance and majesty.

Verses 55-57:
Warning to the opponents:
In the definition of the people of knowledge and faith, He said: "The opposition of the opponents makes it firm in them: what the Prophet is promising and warning is completely truth." Now He said: "As for these deniers, they will remain in doubt regarding this promise and warning in the same manner as they are today. These will believe only when the Resurrection comes upon them suddenly, or the punishment of some ہولناک barren day comes upon them." "'Aqeem" means barren. In Surah Adh-Dhariyat, verse 29, it is said: (She said, "A barren old woman!")—from here, the meaning of unbeneficial and barren is born in it, and it was applied also to that storm wind which creates destruction of Resurrection, but has no aspect of benefit in it. Consequently, the punishment that came upon the people of 'Ad is expressed as "reeh 'aqeem" by the Quran: (And in 'Ad, when We sent upon them the barren wind.) [Adh-Dhariyat: 41]. In Surah Al-Qamar, verse 19, the structure "yawmi nahsin" (a day of ill fortune) is also used. See their details in their places.

"Al-mulku yawma'idhin lillahi yahkumu baynahum"—it is stated: "If these people are waiting only for the manifestation of Resurrection, they must remember: on that day, the entire power and authority will belong to Allah, the One without partners. On that day, neither their assemblies and parties will be of any use to them, nor their assumed partners and intercessors, and that day will be for the decision of reward and punishment and the manifestation of justice, not for effort and action; on that day, Allah will decide between them as to who won and who lost, and everyone will face the results of their actions."

"Falladhina amanoo wa 'amiloo as-salihati fee jannati an-na'eemi walladhina kafaroo wa kadhdhaboo bi-ayatina fa-ola'ika lahum 'adhabun muheen"—this is the proclamation of that decision whose mention passed in the above segment. It is said: "On that day, those of faith and righteous action will enter gardens of pleasure, and those who disbelieved and denied Our verses—for them will be a humiliating punishment." A humiliating punishment because they considered themselves superior to the truth presented by God and His Messenger, which is the worst arrogance; because of this arrogance, they are worthy of: not only being punished in the Hereafter, but being given that punishment which is also humiliating. It should be kept in mind: the severity of this punishment becomes double and multiple because, along with the torture, it also carries the curse of humiliation and disgrace.

The upcoming theme... Verses 58-76:
Forward, first comfort is given to those Muslims who, in this period of trial, were migrating to different regions to save their religion and faith from the oppressions of the enemies of truth. Giving them the reference to His attributes, He assured them: "The entire matter is in the power of Allah. He is observing and hearing everything; just as after hopelessness, the clouds of mercy arise and wash the land, similarly the clouds of mercy will rain upon you too, and you will be made prosperous. The respite God is giving to your enemies is given because God is highly kind to His servants—He does not make haste in giving punishment—but there is a limit to this respite. If they do not appreciate it, they will see its end."

After that, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), He said: "If your opponents continue on the path they have been walking, refer their matter to Allah, and do not put them much in discussion. Soon that day is coming when God will decide their matter. On that day, these deities of theirs will be of no use, at whose criticism their bodies catch fire. If their bodies catch fire, let them; explain to them clearly: all your deities together are not capable of creating even a fly, and if a fly should steal away anything from them, they are not capable of recovering it. Explain to these fools also: the status of the angels is not of God's daughters or His partners, but of His servants. He chooses His messengers from among them too in the manner He chooses from among humans. They are under the encompassment of God at all times. The return of matters and their decision is with Allah alone..." In this light, recite the verses.

In the path of migration, even the first step is in the ruling of the destination:
Now, conjoined to the above glad tidings given to the believers, glad tidings are given specifically to those oppressed Muslims who, constrained by the oppression of the disbelievers of Quraysh, were forced to migrate from Makkah to save their religion and faith. It is said: "Those who migrate in the path of Allah, let them remain assured: in this path, even the first step holds the status of the destination. If they are killed before reaching their place of migration, or death comes to them, they will obtain the status of martyrdom, and Allah will provide them with His good provision." Under Al-Imran 169, it has passed: those killed in God's path do not die but remain alive, and they receive provision in their barzakh life from the table of their Lord. (Rather, they are alive with their Lord, receiving provision.) [Al-Imran: 169]

In the path of migration, even natural death holds the status of martyrdom:
This same glad tiding is given here to those too who set out with the intention of migration and death comes to them, even if they were not killed. As if in this path, being killed alone is not martyrdom, but natural death too is in the ruling of martyrdom. "Rizq" (provision) here, as we have been explaining, is a comprehensive expression of the boundless favors of God. It must not be taken in a limited meaning. Within "wa inna Allaha lahuwa khayru ar-raziqeen" (and indeed, Allah is the best of providers), pointer is made to the reality: when God is the best of providers, who can estimate what provision He will give to those who abandon their homes and everything for His sake!

Verses 59-60:
Great glad tidings for the emigrants:
It is said: "He will admit them into a place with which they will be pleased." The word "yardawnahu" (they will be pleased with it) is very brief, but inside it, a world of meanings is hidden. That is: "The servant who, for the sake of God, leaves his home and homeland—if death comes to him in this path, let him remain assured: God will admit him into such a place where not only all his expectations will be fulfilled, but he will receive what he cannot even imagine in this world."

"Wa inna Allaha la'aleemun haleemun"—meaning, let Our servants remain assured: in this world, what they are suffering at the hands of the enemies of truth for Our sake, We are not unaware of it—We are aware of everything. But along with Al-'Aleem (The All-Knowing), Our attribute is also Al-Haleem (The Forbearing). For this reason, We do not grasp Our enemies immediately; rather, We give them full opportunity for reform. But they must remember: there is delay in Our presence, but not injustice.

In God's presence, there is delay, but not injustice:
"Dhalika wa man 'aqaba bimithli ma 'ooqiba bihi thumma bughiya 'alayhi layansurannahu Allahu inna Allaha la'afuwwun ghafoorun" [60]

An important proclamation:
This "dhalika" (that), as passed in verse 30, stands for the entire sentence. The meaning is: this which has been said is not a light statement, but a highly important reality. Let both friend and enemy hear it well. Furthermore, this proclamation is also made: the oppressed Muslims, if they take an action exactly equal to the oppression on their lives and wealth in defense, have the right to do so. They are given permission for it, and if any further transgression is committed against them after that, let their enemies remember: God is at the back of His servants, and He will surely help them.

In "man 'aqaba bimithli ma 'ooqiba bihi", the structure of similarity is maintained in the manner of "daanahum kama daanoo" or "jaza'u sayyi'atin sayyi'atun mithluha". In "thumma bughiya 'alayhi" (then transgression was committed against him), the expression of the reality is: if, on some defensive action of the Muslims, the disbelievers commit further transgression to depress their courage, so that they may not dare in the future to protect their honor and respect, let the disbelievers remember and the Muslims remain assured: Allah will never let this happen; rather, He will surely help His servants. Within the briefness of "He will surely help them", the detail implied was demonstrated by the subsequent events, and history is witness to it. It should be kept in mind: this glad tiding was given to the Muslims at the time of migration. Later, when the Muslims reached Madinah and became a power, this same thing was said in highly clear words which has passed in verses 39-40. Take a look at them.

The specific context of referencing the attributes of Forgiveness and Pardon:
Within "Inna Allaha la'afuwwun ghafoorun", there is a specific context. This statement, as I have pointed out, was made exactly at the time of migration. For this reason, there was possibility: the Muslims, afflicted at the hands of the disbelievers, might take some hasty step as a retaliatory action. While the appropriate time for it had not yet arrived; for this reason, Allah reminded them of His attributes of Pardon and Forgiveness: "Although you have the right to protect your life, wealth, honor, and respect, and God is at your back, yet in the manner We work with pardon and forgiveness despite people's transgressions, you too must work with pardon and forgiveness for a while. The time to deal with these rebels is arriving." The meaning is: "Your Lord wants to see the reflection of His attributes inside you too."

The first proof of the promise of help:
This is the proof of the above promise of help: "God is not a useless entity in this universe, as these fools have assumed, but the actual administrator is He alone—He alone brings the night after the day and manifests the day after the night, and He is All-Hearing and All-Seeing—meaning, (God forbid) He is not blind and deaf that the wicked and corrupt may commit whatever they wish in His world while He sits unaware. He is observing and hearing everything." So, when the actual administrator is He, and He also observes and hears everything, how will He remain detached from the politics of this universe? It is necessary that He, against those who are creating corruption in His world, help those who are seekers of its reform. And when He is capable of bringing the day after the night, and the observation of this power of His is being done by every person every day, then erasing the darkness of disbelief to illuminate the world with the light of faith—what is difficult for Him!

The second proof of the promise of help:
Let no one remain in this delusion: "After all, at the back of these opponents are some powers too." It is said: "The actual Sovereign in this universe is Allah alone, and these whom they worship and call besides Allah are all nonentities, merely the creation of imagination, and entirely a delusion and deceit. Allah is a highly high, exalted, and great entity. What is the comparison of these stone and clay statues with God? Those who make these things deities and worship them are completely unaware of the majesty and status of God."

The testimony of the horizons in support of the promise of help:
On the address "Alam tara..." (Do you not see?), we have discussed in various places: this comes to draw the attention of every single individual of the addressee group. In the previous verses, Allah has confirmed the promise of help through His attributes. In this verse, along with His attributes, the testimony of the horizons is also included. The meaning is: "Let no one, on seeing the unfavorableness of the current apparent situations, consider the manifestation of God's help far-fetched. No person can understand how the status of God's help will manifest, and from where His hands will appear. You see the earth completely dry and barren, having no trace of greenery and vegetation, nor any sign of clouds on the horizon; but when God wills, His sent winds bring caravans of clouds and spread them in the atmosphere. Then, in the blink of an eye, the entire earth is washed, and within sight, green fields are spread everywhere." Similarly, Allah, whenever He wills, will send His clouds of mercy and help for His servants too, no matter how unfavorable and unhelpful the apparent situations may be.

The reference to the attributes of Subtlety and Acquaintance in a highly subtle manner:
Here, the reference to the attributes of "Lateef" (Subtle) and "Khabeer" (Acquainted) has come in a highly subtle manner. "Lateef" means subtle and reaching the depth. That is: He brings His plans into effect in such a manner that no one has the slightest suspicion of them. He is well-acquainted. People see only the apparent, but He is aware of what is hidden in the veils of the past, and also aware of what is in the cover of the future.

God is independent of all, yet with all:
This is the further confirmation of the above theme: "Whatever is in the heavens and the earth is God's alone and in His control, so who can obstruct His intention? He is independent of all and self-sufficient in His essence." The attribute of "Hameed" (Praiseworthy) here is by way of vanguard—meaning, He is, along with being independent, Praiseworthy too. "Hameed" means: the source of all praiseworthy attributes and actions. The need for this vanguard was so that, because of God's independence, hopelessness might not be born in the servants; rather, they should hope: despite His independence, His favor is running for the creation at all times. He is independent of all, yet with all.

Warning to the rebels:
That is: "The respite man has obtained in this world has been obtained through the favor, grace, kindness, and mercy of God. Every single thing of the heaven and the earth, which is engaged in the service of man, and without which man's survival in this world is not possible for a single moment, is engaged by the command of God. If God's command were not there, every thing of this universe would become man's rebel and his enemy." Look at the ship itself: this is the command of God and His law of subjection that it runs for you, tearing the chest of the sea, otherwise, in the blink of an eye, the entire fleet would drown. It is God alone who is holding the sky lest it fall upon your heads. On the rule of the omitted first noun before "an", discussion has passed in its place.

"Inna Allaha bin-nasi lara'oofun raheemun"—meaning, all these things are witnesses: whatever man has obtained in this world is not based on his own strength or his own science, but is based on the kindness and mercy of God. If Allah were to move even a single brick of this system of the universe slightly from its place, the entire building would collapse.

Keep in view the tone of these sentences. The warning in them for the rebels of Quraysh is prominent from every word. The meaning is: "Do not make haste for Our punishment. If delay is occurring in the punishment, it is occurring not because this is some difficult work for Us, but it is occurring only because of Our kindness and mercy, so that you may benefit from this opportunity to reform your attitude."

The actual cause of rebellion:
This is the reminder of the Hereafter: "If punishment does not come in this world, what is the cause of comfort in this? Forward, the Hereafter is present! Ultimately, the return of all is to Allah alone. God alone gave life, and He alone gives death. Then He alone will raise you alive. When He faced no difficulty in creating you the first time, why should raising you again be difficult for Him!" "Inna al-insana lakafoorun"—this is the actual cause of all this rebellion: "As far as the statements of the Quran and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) are concerned, they are completely clear, having no room for dispute; but these people are highly ungrateful and unappreciative." By the word "al-insan" (man), the pointer of species is to these opponents, but to express aversion, the matter is said with a general word instead of addressing them directly. In this style, there is also the theme of expressing sorrow, to which we have pointed in its place.

On the word "mansak", discussion has passed in verse 34. Here, this word has entered for the method of worship, the apparent structure of the Shariah, and its rules and regulations.

Direction to avoid the debaters:
In this Surah, the details have passed: in this period, the Jews and Christians too were openly supporting the Quraysh, rather they were providing maximum fuel for this debate. For this reason, pointing to them, He said: "We had prescribed for every Ummah a regulation, and the purpose of it was: they should worship God according to this regulation, and when God makes any change in it, accept it with heart and soul, so that this may be the scale of distinction between their sincere ones and rigid ones." Consequently, in the era of every messenger, changes in the apparent structure of the Shariah occurred, as well as the reform of those innovations created by innovators. Those inside whom was the search for truth accepted this reform with a sincere heart, but those who were the worshippers of custom and rituals persisted in their rigidity, and in the bias of their favored things, kept debating against the truth. He said: "This same is the state of these opponents of yours too. These too, due to their rigidity and ignorant bias, are not to abandon the path they have been walking. So, there is no need to pursue them much." "Falayunazi'unnaka fee al-amri"—meaning, do not give them any such opportunity to find any way for debate with you. Just invite them to that straight path of your Lord on which you are walking. If they come, then excellent; if they do not come, leave them to their state. Indeed, you are on a straight path—so for one who is on a straight path, what harm can reach him from others going on a wrong path! This exact same theme, with minor words, has passed in other places in the Quran. Some verses: in Surah Al-Baqarah, at the end of the discussion of Qiblah, it is said:
(And for every one is a direction to which he turns himself; so hasten to [do] good works.) [Al-Baqarah: 148]

In Surah Al-Ma'idah, He said:
