(To each of you We prescribed a law and a method. And if Allah had willed, He would have made you one nation, but [He intended] to test you in what He has given you; so hasten to [do] good works.) [Al-Ma'idah: 48]

Take a look at the commentary of these verses. We have clarified under these verses: this is not a direction of tolerance toward the Jews and Christians, as some have assumed; rather, this is the expression and declaration of detachment from their attitude.

Verses 68-69:
That is: "As far as you are concerned, do not give them any opportunity of debate from your side. But despite this attitude of yours, if they do arrive for debate, just try to dismiss them saying: 'Allah will decide our and your dispute on the Day of Resurrection. He is well-aware of what you are doing.'" The meaning is: "Now do not give them much attention, but refer their matter to Allah. What they are doing, they are doing knowingly, merely out of mischief. These are not people to be convinced by arguments."

Address to the Prophet, but warning directed to the opponents:
In this verse, although the address is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the warning in its every word is directed entirely to the opponents. It is said: "Do you not know that whatever is in the heavens and the earth, God is aware of all? So, refer the matter of these wicked ones to Allah alone—He will deal with them! 'Inna dhalika fee kitabin'—the threat of the previous sentence has become even more sharp in this. That is: let no one assume that these are merely hoaxes; rather, everyone must know that God has preserved everyone's record with complete care, in a register. 'Inna dhalika 'ala Allahi yaseerun'—and let no one remain in this delusion: after all, who can keep the detail of every single individual and every minor thing preserved? For others, this work is indeed impossible, but for Allah, this is very easy."

Dispelling another misunderstanding:
This has dispelled another misunderstanding too: on the day God sits for justice, no one will be able to help these wrongdoers. Those things whom they have made God's partners and intercessors have no reality. Neither has God sent down any decree in their favor that they are partners in His godship, nor do they themselves possess any knowledge regarding them. To claim regarding anyone, without God's permission, that God has made him His partner is a fabrication against Allah, which is a great crime, and to make something a deity regarding which there is no intellectual or natural argument is merely following assumption, and assumption does not stand in place of knowledge and truth.

"Sata yas'too" means to attack and fall upon.

The representation of the idols' nonentity, and the answer to their supporters:
The meaning is: although they have no argument in favor of their deities, their support for them is such that when the highly clear and argued verses are recited to them in favor of Monotheism, their bodies catch fire, their faces become distorted, and their brows become tensed. It appears they will fall upon those who are reciting the verses of Allah to them. It is said: "If they are so angry with the verses of Allah and those who recite them, say to them: 'Shall I inform you of something which will distort your faces far more than these things, and with which you must inevitably deal? It is the fire of Hell! And it is an extremely bad abode.'"

That is: "When you are so angry on hearing the nonentity of your deities, hear a reality-revealing parable of their helplessness. All these deities of yours whom you call besides Allah, if they want to create even a single fly, they cannot create it, even if all of them together use all their strength for it. And not only can they not create a fly, but their helplessness is such that if a fly should steal away anything from the sweet offering you place before them, they are not capable of recovering it. Weak indeed are the pursuer and pursued!"

Within "da'ufa at-talibu wal-matloobu" (weak are the pursuer and pursued), there is a highly subtle and eloquent pointer to the reality: man accepts someone as a deity because by doing so, the remedy for his weakness and helplessness is done, but these fools have made their deities those who are even more weak and helpless than themselves. They seek the remedy for their exhaustion from those poor ones who are not even capable of warding off a fly from their own faces.

They have not estimated the status and greatness of God at all. God is not a helpless entity like their deities; rather, He is an extremely powerful and dominant entity. Whatever He intends, He is fully powerful over executing it, and it is not possible that anyone can obstruct His intention.

The status of the angels:
That is: "These fools starting the worship of the angels, assuming them the daughters of God, is merely the result of their foolishness and unawareness of God's status. The angels are not God's daughters or His partners, but are His servants. If they possess any status, it is: just as He chooses His specific servants from among humans as His prophets, He chooses His specific servants from among the angels as His messengers to His prophets."

From "Inna Allaha samee'un baseerun" (Indeed, Allah is All-Hearing, All-Seeing), pointer is made to two realities simultaneously. One: God Himself is All-Hearing and All-Seeing, hearing and seeing everything, so what is His need to make anyone His partner. Second: when God is All-Hearing and All-Seeing, He is also supervising the duties of these angels at every step; it is not possible that they can deviate by a hair's breadth from His prescribed limits.

Whatever is before and behind these angels is in the encompassment of God's knowledge; for this reason, neither can they add anything to God's knowledge, nor can any of their words and actions be above God's supervision, nor are they in a position to say to God regarding anyone that they have knowledge about him while God does not. All matters are presented in His presence alone. Neither are they presented before these angels, nor will be. Even the matters entrusted to these angels—their report too must be presented by these angels in the presence of God.

The upcoming theme... Verses 77-78:
Conclusion of the Surah:
These are the concluding verses of the Surah. Above, you have seen: proving the disbelievers of Quraysh to be betrayers and traitors, He declared them completely unfit for the Abrahamic inheritance and the guardianship of the House of Allah, and at the same time pointed out: the true heirs of its guardianship are these Muslims who were expelled from there, and gave them permission: they have the right to wage Jihad for the freedom and purification of the House of Allah. Now forward, in the concluding verses, directions are being given to the Muslims which are necessary for performing this great responsibility... See the verses.

Demands of the Muslims' office of leadership:
Above, we pointed out: the demands of that office of leadership are being mentioned, whose glad tidings are given in this Surah. This office is a great honor and also a great responsibility. For this reason, first of all, the command of bowing and prostration was given. Bowing and prostration are the expressions of prayer. But we have pointed under At-Tawbah 112: when prayer is expressed by these words, it points also to the absorption and engagement inside the prayers, and particularly, the night prayers (Tahajjud) are meant by them, whose care is necessary for becoming worthy of great responsibilities. This is a matter of both honor and responsibility; for this reason, from the aspect of gratitude, the direction of prayer was given, and also from the aspect of becoming worthy of the coming responsibility. Take a look at verse 41 of this Surah too, in which the Muslims are told what the requirements of the guardianship of the Sanctuary are, and what their Lord expects from them after honoring them with this office.

"Wa'budoo rabbakum"—this is the mention of the general after the specific, and worship, as we have explained in its place, contains also the meaning of obedience. That is: in all spheres of life, worship and obey God alone.

"Waf'aloo al-khayra"—this is even more general. That is: perform further goodness and righteousness. This is a pointer to those good works and righteousnesses whose status, though not of obligations, is included in virtues and decencies, and they have a great part in reforming life.

"La'allakum tuflihoon"—meaning: "Care for these works, so the capability of that power in the land which is promised to you will arise in you, and also the capability to win the game of the Hereafter."

Direction for practical struggle alongside spiritual preparation:
"Wa jahidoo fee Allahi haqqa jihadihi"—alongside the above moral and spiritual preparation, this is the direction for practical struggle with all life and wealth. By Jihad here is not meant fighting (qital). That is conditional upon conditions and situations. Its mention has passed in verses 38-41. Here, by it is meant every struggle which is within the servant's capability to elevate the word of Allah.

In "fee Allahi", the first noun is omitted—meaning "fee sabeeli Allahi" (in the path of Allah)—and by "haqqa jihadihi" (with its due Jihad), the warning is: this struggle is not desired with half-heartedness and weakness, but the game of life and wealth must be played for it.

"Huwa ijtabakum wa ma ja'ala 'alaykum fee ad-deeni min harajin"—meaning, the God who is demanding this Jihad from you is He who has chosen you to carry the great trust of His perfect religion. Chosen you—meaning, by deposing others, whether the Quraysh or the Jews—so maintain the honor of this choice, and assume the responsibilities of this office of leadership of nations with complete determination. "Wa ma ja'ala 'alaykum fee ad-deeni min harajin"—meaning, in this religion, there are no such restrictions and bands upon you as were in the religion of the Jews. Allah, by His favor, has preserved this religion of nature from such bands. The meaning is: although this burden is great, it is not such that you cannot lift it.

"Millata abeekum Ibraheema"—meaning: "This is the creed of your father Ibrahim, so what other creed can be more desired and beloved for the offspring than the father's creed!" It should be kept in mind: the actual address here is to the children of Ismail, who not only were the offspring of Hazrat Ibrahim but also took great pride in being his offspring. Before "millah", some verb like "wattabi'oo" (and follow) can be assumed as omitted, and "ijtabakum" can be assumed as containing some verb which is appropriate for the context. I have kept in view the verse of Al-Ma'idah: (and I approved for you Islam as a religion) [Al-Ma'idah: 3], assuming the inclusion, and have kept it in view in the translation.

Pointers to the prayer of Hazrat Ibrahim:
"Huwa sammakumu al-muslimeena min qablu wa fee hadha"—this is a pointer to the fact: the creed of Ibrahim is this, because Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) himself named you Muslims before this, and then this same name was chosen for you in this religion too. Some have accepted the referent of "huwa" as Allah, but in my view, this is a pointer to that prayer of Hazrat Ibrahim which has entered in Al-Baqarah:
(Our Lord, and make us submissive [Muslims] to You and from our descendants a submissive nation [Muslims] to You.) [Al-Baqarah: 128]

The purpose of pointing to this name and this prayer is the encouragement of the Muslims: "You are the manifestation of this prayer of your father Ibrahim; remember your history!" Let no one have the misunderstanding from this: the word Islam or Muslim was first used by Hazrat Ibrahim alone! This is the nature of the entire universe: (And to Him submitted whoever is in the heavens and the earth) [Al-Imran: 83], and the religion of all prophets has always been this. However, Hazrat Ibrahim had prayed for a nation to arise from the offspring of Hazrat Ismail, and he had named it the Muslim Ummah beforehand; so when this Ummah manifested, Allah named this Ummah with this name.

"Liyakoona ar-rasoolu shaheedan 'alaykum wa takoonoo shuhada'a 'ala an-nasi"—this exact same theme has passed in Al-Baqarah:
(And similarly We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses over mankind and the Messenger may be a witness over you.) [Al-Baqarah: 143]

This is the purpose of this choice described: "Allah has chosen you, deposing all others, so that just as Allah's Messenger may be a witness over you, you too may be witnesses over mankind regarding God's religion until Resurrection." This is the purpose of your existence and the goal of your choice. On the demands of this testimony, we have discussed under Al-Baqarah 143.

Direction for prayer and Zakah in terms of the responsibility of being witnesses over mankind:
"Faqeemoo as-salata wa atoo az-zakata wa'tasimoo billahi huwa mawlakum fani'ma al-mawla wa ni'ma an-naseer"—in the above verse, the speech began with prayer, and we have explained its aspect. Now, the Surah is concluding with the direction to care for prayer and Zakah. Here, this direction is in terms of the responsibility of being witnesses over mankind placed upon this Ummah. On these two pillars, as we have explained under Al-Baqarah 29, the entire building of religion is established; for this reason, as long as their care is maintained, religion will remain. If their care is finished, religion too will collapse.

The vanguard of the path of testimony of truth:
"Wa'tasimoo billahi" (And hold fast to Allah) is exactly like "wa'tasimoo bihabli Allahi" (hold fast to the rope of Allah). This is the remedy shown for those difficulties which will be faced in the path of the testimony of truth. It is said: "With complete determination, stand firm on your position, remember your Lord, hold His Book with strength, and have full trust in His help and support; He is the best refuge and the best helper!"

On these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. And our final call is: praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
